Trap anharmonicity creates an effective rotating deformation that geometrically squeezes the condensate and synchronizes its intrinsic rotation with orbital motion. Simulations predict longer-time formation of ring-shaped vortex arrays from the coherent rotating matter wave.
main_result: A self-organized locking of condensate rotation to orbital motion is demonstrated, with simulations showing vortex-array formation.
application_angle: The mechanism provides a way to engineer robust circulating states in ultracold-atom platforms relevant to atomtronics and quantum simulation.

title: Self-Pulsing Microring Resonator Networks for Bandwidth-Efficient Event Detection in an Optical Fiber Sensor
abstract: The native processing of time-dependent signals from optical sensors by integrated photonic circuits can potentially bring significant advantages in terms of energy consumption, latency and processing power, as it allows skipping or reducing the use of fast digital electronics and directly exploiting optical degrees of freedom and parallelism. However, due to a short memory, optical operations usually struggle to directly process optical signals with relatively slow (<MHz) dynamics from optical sensors. In this work, we experimentally show that these limitations can be overcome by exploiting the self-pulsing dynamics in a microring resonator (MRR) network. In particular, we demonstrate that such dynamics can expand and retain information about perturbations sensed by a fiber sensor. This reduces the minimum sampling rate for the digitization of the sensor signal by at least one order of magnitude. The reduction is achieved by combining fiber sensing measurements at two different perturbation locations and frequencies with MRR network measurements at multiple output ports, input power levels and laser wavelengths. This work represents a first step in bridging time-dependent optical processing and optical sensing at sub-μs time scales.
one_liner: Self-pulsing microring resonator networks retain fiber-sensor perturbation information and reduce digitization bandwidth needs.
why_it_matters: It shows integrated photonics can preprocess slow sensor dynamics optically, cutting reliance on fast electronics for event detection.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally use self-pulsing dynamics in a microring resonator network to expand and retain information from a time-dependent optical fiber sensor signal. Measurements combine perturbations at two locations and frequencies with multiple optical outputs, powers, and wavelengths. The network lowers the minimum sampling rate for digitizing the sensor signal by at least an order of magnitude.
platform_or_system: microring network with optical fiber sensor
main_result: An integrated self-pulsing microring network reduced required sensor-signal sampling rate by at least 10x.
application_angle: This points toward low-latency, energy-efficient optical front ends for distributed sensing and event detection.

title: Controlled ion-ion interactions and cavity-enhanced emission of a coherent dinuclear Eu$^{3+}$ complex
abstract: Molecular rare-earth-ion complexes offer unique opportunities for quantum technologies by combining the intrinsic coherence properties of rare-earth ions with chemically tunable molecular environments. A crucial capability is the realization of multi-qubit architectures with defined qubit couplings to enable two-qubit quantum gates. Here, we investigate the optical coherence properties and excitation-induced interactions of two Eu$^{3+}$-based molecular complexes, comparing a mononuclear reference system with a dinuclear analogue in which two Eu$^{3+}$ ions are positioned at a well-defined intramolecular distance of about 7 Angstrom. Using cryogenic ensemble spectroscopy, including spectral hole burning, free-induction decay, and photon echo measurements at temperatures down to 100 mK, we demonstrate long optical coherence times $T_{2,\text{o}}$ of up to 9 $μ$s. As a key step toward scalable multi-qubit architectures, a control-target sequence was implemented to probe conditional ion-ion interactions, revealing a stronger interaction-induced dephasing in the dinuclear complex. Finally, we show the integration of the dinuclear complex into a fiber-based optical microcavity, and observe an 380-fold emission enhancement of the $\mathrm{}^5\mathrm{D}_0\rightarrow\mathrm{}^7\mathrm{F}_0$ transition. Together, these results position molecular rare-earth complexes as versatile and chemically tunable building blocks for scalable quantum technologies.
one_liner: A dinuclear Eu3+ molecular complex shows controlled ion-ion interactions and strong cavity-enhanced optical emission.
why_it_matters: Chemically defined rare-earth complexes could become tunable multi-qubit building blocks with optical access and engineered coupling.
detailed_summary: The study compares mononuclear and dinuclear Eu3+ molecular complexes, with the dinuclear system placing two ions about 7 Angstrom apart. Cryogenic spectral hole burning, free-induction decay, and photon echo measurements down to 100 mK show optical coherence times up to 9 microseconds. A control-target sequence reveals stronger interaction-induced dephasing in the dinuclear complex, and fiber microcavity integration enhances emission by 380-fold.
platform_or_system: dinuclear Eu3+ molecular complex in fiber microcavity
main_result: The work demonstrates conditional ion-ion interaction signatures, 9 microsecond optical coherence, and 380-fold cavity emission enhancement.
application_angle: It advances molecular rare-earth systems toward optically addressable, chemically tunable multi-qubit hardware.

title: Super-Heisenberg Non-Equilibrium Quantum Sensing with Waveguide-Coupled Emitters
abstract: We explore an array of quantum emitters as non-equilibrium probes, coupled to a one-dimensional photonic waveguide, aiming to estimate its properties such as wave number which encodes the waveguide frequency and dispersive characteristics. By considering transient dynamics following initial excitation, we show that the quantum Fisher information (QFI) can be significantly enhanced through careful emitter positioning. For two-emitter probes, optimal spacing stabilizes populations and coherences in the single-excitation subspace, suppressing super radiant decay and extending both the magnitude and longevity of QFI. Randomized emitter configurations also reveal that vanishing waveguide-mediated cross decay maximizes both achievable sensitivity and the temporal duration over which information about the parameter remains accessible. Extending to multipartite probes, we demonstrate that the maximum QFI and its temporal integral scale with system size, exceeding the Heisenberg limit for all positioning strategies. Our results highlight the potential of waveguide-coupled emitter arrays as versatile quantum sensors, where collective radiative dynamics can be harnessed to achieve tunable, long-lived, and enhanced precision.
one_liner: Waveguide-coupled emitter arrays can use transient collective dynamics for enhanced, long-lived quantum sensing beyond Heisenberg scaling.
why_it_matters: It links emitter placement and radiative interference to practical sensitivity gains in photonic quantum sensors.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes quantum emitters coupled to a one-dimensional photonic waveguide as non-equilibrium probes of waveguide parameters such as wave number. For two emitters, optimized spacing suppresses superradiant decay and preserves single-excitation coherences, increasing both the magnitude and lifetime of quantum Fisher information. Multipartite arrays show maximum QFI and time-integrated QFI scaling beyond the Heisenberg limit across positioning strategies.
main_result: Collective transient dynamics in emitter arrays are shown to yield long-lived QFI enhancements with super-Heisenberg scaling.
application_angle: The results guide the design of tunable photonic quantum sensors for estimating waveguide dispersion and frequency-related parameters.

title: Dark state spectroscopy in nonlinear waveguide quantum electrodynamics
abstract: Quantum systems face a fundamental trade-off: they must remain decoupled from the environment to maintain long coherence times, yet they require interactions with the environment to be accessible for measurement. As a prime example, emitter arrays coupled to waveguides facilitate collective modes that, owing to interference, can suppress radiation into the waveguide. While complete destructive interference creates perfectly dark states with infinite lifetimes, their inherent decoupling makes them unmeasurable in standard waveguide quantum electrodynamics. Consequently, current approaches must rely on system non-idealities that permit measurement but limit the coherence times. In this work, we lift this limitation by proposing the use of weakly squeezed light generated in \{chi}(2) nonlinear waveguides for the spectroscopy of completely dark states. We show that the fluorescence spectrum probes transitions between the dressed dark states of the emitter array. This work paves the way towards the measurement and control of dark states, with applications for robust quantum memories, computation, and communication.
one_liner: Weak squeezed light in nonlinear waveguides is proposed as a spectroscopy tool for otherwise inaccessible dark states.
why_it_matters: It offers a route to measure and control perfectly dark collective states without sacrificing their protected coherence.
detailed_summary: The work proposes dark-state spectroscopy using weakly squeezed light generated in chi(2) nonlinear waveguides. In emitter arrays coupled to waveguides, perfectly dark states are normally protected but inaccessible to standard measurement because they decouple from radiation. The authors show that fluorescence can probe transitions between dressed dark states, enabling access without relying on damaging non-idealities.
main_result: The proposal shows that squeezed-light fluorescence spectroscopy can access transitions between dressed perfectly dark states.
application_angle: This could enable protected quantum memories, controllable waveguide-QED registers, and photonic interfaces for quantum communication.

title: Measurement-Free Toric-Code Memory in Array Globally Controlled Rydberg Array
abstract: The central prerequisite of any fault-tolerant quantum architecture is a quantum memory: a block of encoded physical qubits whose logical state is actively preserved against noise across many rounds of error correction. In neutral-atom Rydberg arrays, realizing such a memory is obstructed not by the entangling gates themselves, which are already fast and high-fidelity, but by the auxiliary operations that a conventional error-correction cycle requires: mid-circuit fluorescence measurement, inter-zone atom transport, and locally focused single-qubit addressing. Each of these introduces latency, atom loss, or optical crosstalk that exceeds the cost of the underlying gates by orders of magnitude. These costs accumulate cycle after cycle, progressively degrading the very logical information the code is meant to protect. Here we propose a protocol that stabilizes a toric-code quantum memory without moving, measuring or local addressing atoms. The key is to use a three-species Rydberg atom array for the complete stabilizer cycle, including syndrome extraction, coherent correction, and ancilla reset, under global, species-selective laser pulses. Numerical simulation of a $4 \times 4$ rotated toric code shows a longer qubit lifetime when the physical error rate is below a pseudo-threshold $p^\star \approx 0.034$. The scheme offers a concrete, hardware-efficient route to topological quantum memory in neutral-atom platforms.
one_liner: Proposes a globally controlled three-species Rydberg-array toric-code memory with no mid-circuit measurement, transport, or local addressing.
why_it_matters: It targets the dominant overheads in neutral-atom error correction by replacing measurement-heavy cycles with coherent, species-selective global control.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a toric-code memory protocol for neutral-atom Rydberg arrays using three atomic species and only global, species-selective laser pulses. Stabilizer extraction, coherent correction, and ancilla reset are performed without atom motion, fluorescence readout, or locally focused addressing. Simulations of a 4 x 4 rotated toric code show logical lifetime improvement below a pseudo-threshold of about 0.034 physical error rate.
main_result: A measurement-free toric-code memory cycle is numerically shown to extend qubit lifetime below a pseudo-threshold p* ≈ 0.034.
application_angle: This is a concrete route toward lower-latency topological memory in scalable neutral-atom quantum processors.

title: Shadow Engineering of Quantum Processes
abstract: Characterizing quantum processes is essential for hardware benchmarking, error diagnosis, and algorithm verification. While recent work [PRX QUANTUM \textbf{4}, 040337 (2023)] extended classical shadows from quantum state to quantum process, enabling efficient single-channel $\mathcal{E}$ property prediction, its applicability to composite processes $f(\mathcal{E}_1, \mathcal{E}_2,\cdots, \mathcal{E}_k)$ remains unexplored. We introduce shadow engineering, a framework encoding the classical shadows of processes into sparse transfer matrices to predict $f(\mathcal{E}_1, \mathcal{E}_2,\cdots, \mathcal{E}_k)$ properties with proven polynomial sample complexity, matching single-channel efficiency while exponentially lower than quantum process tomography. Crucially, this approach repurposes existing $\mathcal{E}_m$-shadow data without physical execution of $f(\mathcal{E}_1, \mathcal{E}_2,\cdots, \mathcal{E}_k)$, enabling flexible quantum process characterization with minimal hardware overhead. We demonstrate the framework's effectiveness and practicality on a superconducting quantum processor for typical applications such as error mitigation and Hamiltonian dynamical simulation. This framework unlocks new capabilities for predicting complex quantum behaviors without physical re-execution, with immediate applications in near-term device calibration and quantum simulation.
one_liner: Introduces shadow engineering to predict properties of composite quantum processes from existing process-shadow data.
why_it_matters: It can benchmark, diagnose, and simulate composed device behavior without physically re-running the full composite process or doing tomography.
detailed_summary: The work encodes process shadows into sparse transfer matrices to estimate properties of composite maps f(E1,...,Ek). The method has polynomial sample complexity and reuses already collected process-shadow data, avoiding full quantum process tomography and avoiding execution of the composed process. It is demonstrated on a superconducting quantum processor for error mitigation and Hamiltonian dynamics simulation tasks.
main_result: The authors demonstrate efficient prediction of composite quantum-process properties from existing single-process shadow data.
application_angle: This can reduce hardware time for calibration, benchmarking, error diagnosis, and near-term quantum simulation workflows.

title: A Geometric Family of Correlations Containing the Quantum Singlet
abstract: We introduce a geometrically constrained hidden-variable framework that generates a family of correlations parametrized by a boundary function, within which the quantum singlet correlation appears as a particular member. Exact expressions for the correlation function are derived. Several structural results are established, including admissibility conditions, symmetry properties, a universal stationary point of the associated CHSH function, and an exact relation between the CHSH value at $ν=π/4$ and a geometric contrast measure defined on the underlying hidden-variable distributions. Rather than treating the quantum singlet correlation as an isolated target to be reproduced, the present framework places it within a broader geometric structure of correlations. These results suggest the existence of a nontrivial geometric structure underlying the family of correlations and motivate the search for a principle capable of selecting the quantum singlet solution from within that family.
one_liner: Develops a geometric hidden-variable correlation family in which the quantum singlet correlation appears as one special case.
why_it_matters: The result is mainly foundational, but it sharpens how singlet-like correlations sit inside broader geometric correlation structures.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs a geometrically constrained hidden-variable framework parameterized by a boundary function and derives exact correlation expressions. It proves structural properties including admissibility conditions, symmetries, a universal stationary point of the CHSH function, and a relation between CHSH value and geometric contrast. The quantum singlet is embedded as a member of this broader family rather than treated as an isolated target.
main_result: The authors derive a family of exact correlations containing the quantum singlet and characterize its CHSH-related structure.
application_angle: Its relevance is indirect for photonic Bell-test and quantum-correlation design, providing theory that may guide interpretation of singlet-correlation experiments.

title: Modeling of Dark Count Probability in Perimeter-Gated SPADs
abstract: This Letter presents a novel analytical framework showing that the dark count probability (PDC) of perimeter-gated single-photon avalanche diodes (pg-SPADs) follows a complementary Gompertz function. Specifically, we show that PDC follows a complementary Gompertz form from which we derive a pixel-specific descriptor, the midpoint perimeter gate voltage, which characterizes a pixel's equiprobable operating point. We further show that a perimeter gate voltage compensation rate may be obtained from this descriptor to offset temperature-induced changes in the pixel's activation function. The proposed framework is experimentally validated using 4,096 pg-SPADs arranged in a 64 x 64 array and manufactured in a 0.35 $μ$m CMOS process. The devices were characterized at temperatures ranging from -5 $^o$C to 55 $^o$C and perimeter gate voltage magnitudes of 0 to 5 V. The measured results demonstrate deterministic bias control of dark count probability across process and temperature variations.
one_liner: Models dark-count probability in perimeter-gated SPADs with a complementary Gompertz law validated on a 64 x 64 CMOS array.
why_it_matters: The model enables pixel-level bias compensation for dark counts across fabrication variation and temperature drift.
detailed_summary: The paper derives an analytical complementary Gompertz model for dark-count probability in perimeter-gated single-photon avalanche diodes. It introduces the midpoint perimeter gate voltage as a pixel-specific descriptor and uses it to compute a compensation rate for temperature-induced activation shifts. Experiments on 4,096 pg-SPADs in a 0.35 um CMOS process from -5 C to 55 C validate deterministic dark-count control.
main_result: A complementary Gompertz model accurately captures pg-SPAD dark-count probability and supports deterministic bias compensation.
application_angle: This directly improves calibration and stability of single-photon imagers, LiDAR receivers, fluorescence sensors, and time-resolved optical instruments.

title: Clifford disentanglers for entanglement reduction in molecular electronic structure simulations
abstract: Entanglement is a key bottleneck limiting the efficiency of tensor-network and quantum simulations of molecular electronic structures. Here, we systematically assess and extend Clifford disentanglers as a structure-preserving approach to entanglement reduction: they can modify the entanglement structure of qubit wavefunctions while retaining the Pauli-string form of qubit Hamiltonians. To enable a practical search over Clifford transformations, we classify Clifford operators by their action on the Schmidt spectrum across a bipartition, reducing the two- and four-qubit search spaces to 20 and 91392 representatives, respectively. Embedded in an iterative Clifford-augmented matrix product state framework, these transformations reduce the energy errors at fixed bond dimension for the molecular test cases studied and mitigate the dependence on orbital orderings and fermion-to-qubit mappings. We further show that Clifford disentanglers can also benefit quantum simulations such as the shallow-circuit variational quantum eigensolver calculations. Together, these results establish Clifford disentanglers as a useful structure-preserving entanglement-engineering tool for tensor-network and quantum simulations of molecular electronic structure, while also clarifying their correlation dependence and motivating future developments.
one_liner: Uses Clifford disentanglers to reduce molecular-simulation entanglement while preserving Pauli-string Hamiltonian structure.
why_it_matters: Lower entanglement can make tensor-network and shallow quantum simulations more accurate at fixed resources.
detailed_summary: The paper classifies Clifford operators by their effect on Schmidt spectra to make searches over two- and four-qubit disentanglers tractable. Embedded in a Clifford-augmented matrix-product-state workflow, the transformations reduce fixed-bond-dimension energy errors across molecular test cases. The authors also show benefits for shallow-circuit VQE-style quantum simulations while retaining Pauli-string Hamiltonian structure.
main_result: Clifford disentanglers reduce simulation energy errors and sensitivity to orbital ordering and fermion-to-qubit mapping.
application_angle: The technique can lower resource requirements for quantum chemistry workloads on near-term quantum hardware and hybrid tensor-network pipelines.

title: Low-symmetry lattices of non-chiral meta-atoms for resonant handedness-preserving reflection
abstract: Mirrors that preserve the handedness of optical radiation upon reflection are an essential building block for the design of numerous resonant nanophotonic structures with capabilities for enantiomeric discrimination. Ordinary metallic and Bragg dielectric mirrors are not suitable in these context since they flip handedness of electromagnetic field upon reflection around normal incidence. While there has been considerable progress in the development of such reflecting structures, this research area remains largely unexplored. Here, we present a detailed numerical and theoretical analysis of the potential of low-symmetry periodic lattices composed of high-symmetry non-chiral meta-atoms for resonant reflection with handedness preservation (HP). Using full-wave numerical simulations, we analyze a family of rhombic and monoclinic (oblique) lattices of circular dielectric disks and/or holes, and in each identify the regime of near-perfect HP reflection. We study the robustness of these structures to geometric deviations, material losses, and incidence angle. Finally, we describe the resonant HP response of these structures using the coupled-mode theory formalism.
one_liner: Shows low-symmetry non-chiral metasurface lattices can provide near-perfect handedness-preserving resonant reflection.
why_it_matters: Handedness-preserving mirrors are key components for resonant chiral nanophotonics and enantiomer-sensitive optical systems.
detailed_summary: The paper numerically and theoretically studies rhombic and monoclinic lattices made from high-symmetry, non-chiral dielectric disks or holes. Full-wave simulations identify regimes with near-perfect handedness-preserving reflection and assess robustness to geometry errors, material loss, and incidence angle. A coupled-mode theory description is developed for the resonant handedness-preserving response.
main_result: Near-perfect resonant handedness-preserving reflection is identified in low-symmetry lattices of non-chiral meta-atoms.
application_angle: These mirrors could support compact chiral sensors, enantiomeric discrimination platforms, and resonant polarization-preserving photonic devices.

title: On-Chip Quantum Randomness Amplification
abstract: Randomness amplification, the task of extracting uniform private bits from biased seeds that may be partly known by a malicious third party, is of central importance in cryptography. The highest security in this task is provided by a class of quantum protocols known as device-independent, which however are challenging to integrate into scalable devices. Semi-device-independent (SDI) protocols are a promising alternative that guarantees security under few natural assumptions, such as bounds on the amount of energy used by the devices. Here, we provide the first demonstration of SDI randomness amplification on an integrated silicon photonic chip, achieving a throughput rate of 20 Mbps suitable for practical applications. This rate is achieved through a novel technique for SDI entropy certification, which delivers strictly tighter von Neumann entropy bounds compared to existing methods and remains valid even if the preparation and measurement devices share quantum correlations. Overall, the methods developed in this work enable the integration of SDI technology into portable telecom devices, opening up a new generation of quantum cryptographic hardware.
one_liner: Silicon photonic chip demonstrates semi-device-independent randomness amplification at 20 Mbps.
why_it_matters: It moves certified private randomness from bulky device-independent setups toward portable, telecom-compatible quantum cryptographic hardware.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates semi-device-independent randomness amplification on an integrated silicon photonic chip. It achieves 20 Mbps throughput using a tighter von Neumann entropy certification method that remains valid even with quantum correlations between preparation and measurement devices. The protocol assumes bounded device energy rather than full device trust.
main_result: The system generates certified amplified private randomness at 20 Mbps on chip.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to compact quantum random number generators and deployable cryptographic modules.

title: Experimental straintronics in nanotube quantum dots
abstract: Single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) are narrow ribbons of graphene with atomically precise edges and a single quantum transport channel, at experimentally-relevant dopings. This makes them ideal systems to harness quantum transport straintronics (QTS), i.e. using mechanical strain to control accurately quantum transport. We present QTS data from three single-wall carbon nanotube quantum dot (SWCNT-QD) transistors over a broad range of in-situ tunable and reversible uniaxial strain ($Δ\varepsilon_\text{mech}\approx$ 0 to 3 %). We first present the nanofabrication of the suspended SWCNT transistors whose channel lengths are $\approx$ 30 nm. The channels are strained by moving gold clamps holding firmly the nanotubes. We present detailed charge transport data, $dI/dV_{\text{B}} - V_{\text{B}} - V_{\text{G}}$ and $dI/dV_{\text{B}} - V_{\text{B}} - Δ\varepsilon_\text{mech}$, showing a large mechanical-gating effect of the SWCNT-QDs. The precise reversibility of the data, and their agreement with QTS theory, confirms that the tubes are strained elastically. We demonstrate that the mechanical control of the QD doping is not due to capacitive-gating effects, but to quantitatively predictable bandstructure changes including a strain-tunable bandgap. This precise mechanical control of the doping and bandgap of SWCNT-QDs could find applications in qubits, condensed matter physics, and homojunction molecular transistors.
one_liner: Suspended carbon-nanotube quantum dots show reversible strain control of doping and bandgap.
why_it_matters: Mechanically tunable quantum transport offers a practical control knob for nanotube qubits, quantum devices, and molecular-scale transistors.
detailed_summary: The paper reports transport measurements on three suspended single-wall carbon nanotube quantum-dot transistors with in-situ reversible uniaxial strain from about 0 to 3 percent. Differential conductance maps show strong mechanical gating caused by strain-driven bandstructure changes rather than capacitive effects. The observed behavior matches quantum transport straintronics theory and indicates elastic deformation of the nanotubes.
main_result: The experiments demonstrate quantitatively predictable mechanical tuning of nanotube quantum-dot doping and bandgap.
application_angle: Strain control could support mechanically programmable nanotube qubits, model quantum devices, and homojunction molecular electronics.

title: Quantum Occam Learning: Sample-Supported Expressibility for Circuit-Based Quantum Learning
abstract: A central principle in quantum machine learning is that an ansatz should be expressive enough to represent the quantum data of interest. Yet, the expressibility is statistically meaningful only insofar as it can be learned from finitely many copies of an unknown quantum state. In this work, we develop an information-theoretic Occam theory for quantum data generated by finite-size quantum circuits. For the class $S_{n,G}$ of $n$-qubit pure states preparable with at most $G$ two-qubit gates, a metric-entropy argument gives the realizable sample law $\widetildeΘ(G/ε^2)$ in the circuit-limited regime. For an arbitrary source $\hatρ$, we introduce the best $G$-gate approximation error $d_G(\hatρ)$ and the approximate circuit complexity $C_η(\hatρ)$. We prove an agnostic quantum Occam theorem: with $M$ copies, one can learn up to the best $G$-gate approximation error plus a statistical penalty $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{G/M})$. We then remove the need to know $G$ in advance through an adaptive model-selection theorem whose oracle inequality selects the circuit complexity justified by the data. Matching lower bounds yield a sample-supported expressibility law: at trace-distance accuracy $ε$, $M$ samples can support only $G_{\rm supported} \simeq Mε^2$ gates, up to logarithmic factors and tomography saturation at $2^n$. Thus, the circuit complexity becomes an adaptive statistical resource rather than a static promise. Our framework turns bounded circuit complexity into a model-selection principle for quantum machine learning.
one_liner: Quantum Occam theory links learnable circuit expressibility to finite-copy sample support.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when circuit-based quantum learning claims are statistically justified, turning ansatz complexity into a data-limited resource.
detailed_summary: This theory paper develops information-theoretic learning bounds for quantum states generated by finite-size circuits. It shows that states preparable with at most G two-qubit gates have a realizable sample law scaling roughly as G over epsilon squared, and gives agnostic bounds including best G-gate approximation error. An adaptive model-selection theorem removes the need to fix G in advance and yields a sample-supported expressibility law.
main_result: The main result is a set of upper and lower bounds showing that M samples support only about M epsilon squared gates, up to logarithmic factors.
application_angle: The framework can guide practical ansatz selection and benchmarking for quantum machine learning on finite-copy quantum data.

title: Time-Frequency Grid States for Reconstruction and Correction of Channel-Induced Distortion in Entangled Photons
abstract: Characterization of time-frequency (TF) quantum states requires reliable reconstruction of their TF distributions. However, imperfect transmission or measurement channels can distort reconstructed joint spectral intensities (JSIs), especially when the underlying perturbation mechanism is unknown. Here, we experimentally demonstrate a reconstruction and correction framework that uses a TF grid state as an intrinsic frequency-domain reference. By analyzing the displacement of the grid points, a Gaussian process regression model is employed to reconstruct a correction mapping for the nonlinear coordinate deformation without assuming a prior physical model of the distortion. The learned mapping reduces the residual coordinate deviation of the TF grid state by approximately a factor of 11 and, when applied to an independent frequency-entangled test state, improves the Gaussian-shape fidelity from 76.2\% to 90.0\%. These results establish TF grid states as practical metrological resources for diagnosing and correcting distortions in TF quantum systems, providing a pathway toward distortion-resilient quantum communication and high-dimensional quantum information processing.
one_liner: Time-frequency grid states calibrate and correct nonlinear distortions in entangled-photon spectra.
why_it_matters: It provides a model-free calibration tool for high-dimensional photonic quantum communication affected by channel or measurement distortions.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a time-frequency grid state as an intrinsic frequency-domain reference for reconstructing channel-induced coordinate distortions. Displacements of grid points train a Gaussian process regression map without assuming a physical model of the distortion. The correction reduces residual coordinate deviation by about 11x and improves an independent entangled test state's Gaussian-shape fidelity from 76.2 percent to 90.0 percent.
main_result: The demonstrated framework reconstructs and corrects nonlinear spectral-coordinate deformation using a TF grid-state reference.
application_angle: It enables distortion-resilient characterization and operation of high-dimensional photonic links for quantum communication.

title: Multipartite reference-frame-independent quantum cryptographic communication
abstract: Reference frame mismatch among communication parties introduces errors in quantum cryptographic protocols. As the number of participants increases, aligning reference frames becomes increasingly difficult, complicating multipartite quantum cryptographic implementations. Here, we theoretically and experimentally investigate multipartite reference-frame-independent (RFI) quantum cryptographic communication using Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states. We generalize the bipartite RFI security parameter $C$ to an $N$-party parameter $C_N$ and derive the asymptotic secret key rate expressed solely in terms of experimentally accessible quantities. We analyze the key rate under global and local depolarizing noise models and find that increasing the number of parties $N$ enhances robustness against global depolarizing noise while increasing vulnerability to local channel noise. We also present a proof-of-principle experimental demonstration of four-party RFI quantum cryptographic communication using four-photon GHZ states, confirming the reference-frame invariance of both the $C_4$ parameter and the secret key rate under various reference frame rotations.
one_liner: Four-photon GHZ experiment demonstrates multipartite reference-frame-independent quantum cryptographic communication.
why_it_matters: It reduces the need for shared frame alignment in multi-user quantum networks, a major practical obstacle for multipartite protocols.
detailed_summary: The paper generalizes bipartite reference-frame-independent security to an N-party parameter C_N and derives an asymptotic secret key rate based on measurable quantities. Noise analysis shows increased robustness to global depolarizing noise with more parties but higher sensitivity to local channel noise. A four-party proof-of-principle experiment with four-photon GHZ states confirms invariance of C_4 and the secret key rate under frame rotations.
main_result: The work demonstrates four-party RFI quantum cryptographic communication with frame-invariant security metrics.
application_angle: This supports scalable multi-party quantum cryptography where precise reference-frame alignment is costly or unstable.

title: An iterative Ising decoder for quantum error correction codes
abstract: The Ising framework maps the decoding problem in quantum error correction onto ground-state optimization of a classical Hamiltonian, in which $X$-$Z$ error correlations enter as cross terms. Under phenomenological depolarizing noise, the exact joint formulation contains up to 8-body interactions for the toric code and 10-body for the $6.6.6$ color code. These high-order terms degrade solver convergence, inflate runtime, and raise the auxiliary spin overhead when embedding into native 2-body Ising hardware. In this work, we propose the iterative low-order decoding (ILOD) algorithm, which alternates between $X$- and $Z$-type sub-Hamiltonians, approximating cross-type correlations through Bayesian priors that reweight each type's couplings using the other type's inferred error configuration. This halves the maximum body count of interaction terms in the Hamiltonian, accelerating the solver, restoring convergence at larger code distances, and reducing the total spin count for 2-body embedding by a factor of $2.5$. For the toric code, ILOD attains a threshold of $4.73%$ versus $4.83%$ for the joint formulation, with the empirical runtime ratio scaling as $(0.81)^d$. For the $6.6.6$ color code, their thresholds agree within statistical uncertainty for small code distances, and ILOD remains convergent for larger distances where the joint formulation fails to converge despite a larger annealing budget.
one_liner: Iterative low-order Ising decoding accelerates correlated quantum error correction decoding with near-joint thresholds.
why_it_matters: Reducing high-order decoding Hamiltonians makes Ising and annealing-based decoders more practical for larger QEC codes.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes iterative low-order decoding, which alternates between X- and Z-type sub-Hamiltonians and passes Bayesian priors between them to approximate cross-type error correlations. This halves maximum interaction body count, improves convergence, and reduces 2-body embedding spin overhead by a factor of 2.5. For the toric code it reaches a 4.73 percent threshold versus 4.83 percent for the full joint formulation, while scaling faster empirically with distance.
main_result: The decoder preserves nearly the same thresholds as joint Ising decoding while lowering Hamiltonian order, runtime, and embedding overhead.
application_angle: It is relevant to practical decoders for surface-like codes on classical Ising solvers or dedicated optimization hardware.

title: Deterministic Single-Photon Emitter Arrays in Hexagonal Boron Nitride by Carbon-Assisted Focused Ion Beam Engineering
abstract: The realization of on-chip photonic circuits requires scalable and deterministic single-photon emitters (SPEs) at room temperature, which remain a challenge in van der Waals materials. In this work, we report a novel three-step fabrication process for the generation of spatially controlled SPE arrays in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). The process comprises site-selective gallium (Ga) focused ion beam milling, nanoscale conformal carbon deposition over the patterned regions, and subsequent thermal annealing. The synergistic combination of these steps resulted in a site-correlated emitter yield of ($\sim 89\%$) across 100 fabrication sites. Second-order autocorrelation measurements revealed pronounced three-level emitter dynamics where the best emitters exhibited high purity ($g^{(2)}(0)=0.15 \pm 0.09$).To the best of our knowledge, this is the first lithography-free, direct-write approach combining Ga-ion milling, selective carbon engineering, and thermal annealing to deterministically generate \hBN{} \SPE{}s. The reproducibility of the method is validated across multiple independently fabricated samples. These results establish a scalable, lithography-free pathway toward on-demand SPE arrays relevant to integrated quantum photonics.
one_liner: Carbon-assisted Ga FIB processing creates deterministic room-temperature hBN single-photon emitter arrays with about 89% site yield.
why_it_matters: Deterministic, lithography-free placement of hBN emitters directly addresses a key bottleneck for scalable integrated quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a three-step direct-write process combining site-selective gallium focused ion beam milling, conformal nanoscale carbon deposition, and thermal annealing in hexagonal boron nitride. Across 100 fabrication sites, the authors report about 89% site-correlated single-photon emitter yield, with autocorrelation measurements showing three-level dynamics and best-emitter purity of g2(0)=0.15 +/- 0.09. The method is validated across multiple independently fabricated samples and avoids conventional lithography.
main_result: The paper demonstrates deterministic generation of spatially controlled hBN SPE arrays with approximately 89% yield and high-purity room-temperature emission.
application_angle: The process offers a scalable route to on-demand quantum light sources for chip-integrated photonic circuits.

title: Partitioned Iterative Quantum Scheduling of Satellites for Urgent Disaster Response: Case study of Wildfire
abstract: The standard in Earth-observation tasks today is having near real-time access to surface images in response to changing conditions. For instance, as urban environments interface more with wildlands and wildfires become less predictable, their tracking with satellite resources becomes essential. This requires the coordination of increasingly large constellations of satellites, giving rise to challenging computational problems. With wildfire detection and tracking as a backdrop, we investigate the power of special purpose and novel computing paradigms to tackle the ensuing satellite scheduling problems, making a compelling case for quantum algorithms. We bring quantum scheduling algorithms closer to implementation by examining both the emerging iterative quantum algorithm framework, which comes with analytic guarantees compared to some classical algorithms, and distributed quantum computing methods whose relevance is on the rise as utility-scale problems begin to get solved with quantum computers. Drawing strength from several computing fronts, we develop a distributed/parallelization scheme in conjunction with the quantum algorithm design and apply these techniques to real-world datasets for wildfire detection. While our quantum subprocesses are currently too small to see significant quantum advantage, our results validate the utility of these techniques, and continue forging the path toward distributed quantum computing.
one_liner: A partitioned iterative quantum scheduling workflow targets satellite tasking for wildfire response using real Earth-observation datasets.
why_it_matters: It connects quantum optimization and distributed quantum computing ideas to an urgent, operational satellite scheduling problem.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates urgent wildfire-monitoring satellite scheduling as a target for iterative quantum algorithms and distributed parallelization. It combines analytic-guarantee quantum scheduling frameworks with partitioning schemes intended to make larger real-world instances more accessible. Experiments on wildfire-related datasets show practical validation of the workflow, though the quantum subprocesses remain too small to demonstrate advantage.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a partitioned quantum-algorithm scheduling pipeline on real-world wildfire detection datasets.
application_angle: The approach is relevant to future quantum-assisted coordination of large satellite constellations for disaster response.

title: Quantum repeater segment with free-space coupled co-trapped ions using telecom photon interference
abstract: A quantum repeater segment is a basic building block of a quantum repeater, generating buffered entanglement of quantum memories to connect quantum repeater cells. It also enables the connection between quantum computers. In the implementation we present here, photons emitted from two co-trapped free-space coupled $^{40}$Ca$^+$ ions are converted to the telecom-C band and interfered after transmission over 440$\,$m of optical fiber (220$\,$m per arm), where a photonic Bell measurement is performed to create entanglement between the memories. With this scheme we generate an entangled $\left|Ψ^+\right\rangle$ Bell state with $\ge 68(8)\,$% fidelity, highlighting trapped $^{40}$Ca$^+$ ions as a promising quantum repeater hardware platform.
one_liner: Co-trapped calcium ions are entangled over fiber via telecom-converted photon interference, realizing a quantum repeater segment.
why_it_matters: It combines trapped-ion memories, free-space photon collection, telecom conversion, and fiber interference in a repeater-relevant hardware demonstration.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses two co-trapped free-space-coupled 40Ca+ ions as quantum memories and converts their emitted photons to the telecom C-band. The photons travel through 440 m of optical fiber total, with 220 m per arm, and are interfered in a photonic Bell measurement. This heralds an entangled Psi+ Bell state between the ions with fidelity at least 68(8)%, demonstrating the elements of a repeater segment.
main_result: The work generates heralded ion-ion entanglement through telecom photon interference after fiber transmission.
application_angle: It advances trapped-ion hardware for quantum repeaters and networked quantum computers using telecom-compatible photonic links.

title: Fermions are fundamentally more nonlocal than Bosons
abstract: Bell's theorem shows that entangled quantum particles can exhibit correlations that classical particles cannot reproduce without an additional nonlocal resource, such as communication. In this sense, quantum particles are fundamentally more nonlocal than classical ones, and entanglement becomes unavoidable in physics. Here we prove the analogous result within quantum theory itself: indistinguishable fermions transmitted through a quantum network can generate correlations that distinguishable particles or indistinguishable bosons cannot reproduce without additional communication. In the same sense, fermions are fundamentally more nonlocal than bosons or distinguishable particles, motivating fermionic anticommutation and indistinguishability as unavoidable operational resources. Our result further implies that fermions can strictly surpass all qubit-based protocols for certain distributed computing tasks, demonstrating that a complete understanding of information processing requires going beyond qubits to fermionic information carriers - febits.
one_liner: The paper proves that indistinguishable fermions can generate network correlations beyond bosons, distinguishable particles, and qubit protocols.
why_it_matters: It elevates fermionic exchange statistics into an operational resource for distributed information processing, not just a physical constraint.
detailed_summary: The authors prove an operational separation showing that indistinguishable fermions sent through a quantum network can create correlations not reproducible by distinguishable particles or indistinguishable bosons without extra communication. The result is framed as an analogue of Bell-style nonlocality within quantum theory itself. It further implies that fermionic information carriers can outperform all qubit-based protocols for certain distributed computing tasks.
main_result: The main result is a proof that fermionic indistinguishability and anticommutation provide strictly stronger nonlocal resources than bosonic or qubit carriers in specific network tasks.
application_angle: The work motivates fermionic information carriers as a practical resource class for future distributed quantum protocols.

title: Quantifying the Distribution of Biexciton Emission Efficiencies in Colloidal Quantum Shells
abstract: The efficiency of multi-photon emission is an important characteristic of quantum light sources. Bright multi-photon emission is desirable for high-power lighting and lasers, while its complete suppression is required for high-purity single-photon generation. In colloidal quantum emitters, multi-photon emission can vary significantly between individual particles. Resolving this heterogeneity remains challenging with conventional particle-by-particle approaches. Here, we introduce a crosstalk-suppressed SPAD-array photon-correlation approach for high-throughput quantification of multi-photon emission from more than 1000 colloidal quantum shells. By projecting two images of the same sample onto distant regions of the detector array, we avoid short-range crosstalk between detector pixels. Time gating suppresses dark-count coincidences and distinguishes individual emitters from clusters. Applying this method to quantum shells reveals a near-Gaussian distribution of biexciton emission efficiencies, with a mean of 0.55 and an estimated intrinsic standard deviation of 0.12. Intra-batch correlations between the biexciton efficiency and the particle brightness are consistent with the volume scaling of Auger quenching. These results establish SPAD-array photon correlation as a scalable route to resolve multi-photon heterogeneities in nanoparticle ensembles.
one_liner: A crosstalk-suppressed SPAD-array correlation method measures biexciton efficiency distributions across over 1000 quantum shells.
why_it_matters: High-throughput photon-correlation metrology can reveal emitter heterogeneity that single-particle studies miss or measure too slowly.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a SPAD-array photon-correlation technique that projects two images of the same sample onto distant detector regions to suppress short-range pixel crosstalk. Time gating reduces dark-count coincidences and separates individual emitters from clusters. Applied to more than 1000 colloidal quantum shells, the method finds a near-Gaussian biexciton efficiency distribution with mean 0.55 and intrinsic standard deviation about 0.12, with brightness correlations consistent with Auger quenching volume scaling.
main_result: The paper demonstrates scalable, high-throughput quantification of biexciton emission efficiencies across more than 1000 emitters.
application_angle: The method supports screening and engineering nanoparticle quantum light sources for either bright multiphoton emission or suppressed multiphoton noise.

title: New bounds on private simultaneous quantum message passing
abstract: In the private simultaneous message (PSM) setting, $k$ players obtain inputs $x_i\in\{0,1\}^n$ and then each send messages to a referee, who should learn $f(x_1,...,x_k)$ but no other information about $(x_1,...,x_k)$. The PSM setting was introduced as a minimal model for secure multiparty computation and has connections to Boolean function complexity. In the quantum setting, PSM has been related to non-local quantum computation (NLQC). The communication and correlation cost of implementing PSM remains poorly understood. Here, we give new upper and lower bounds on the (quantum) PSM model. For lower bounds, we show: 1) Nečiporuk's measure lower bounds the entanglement required for $k$-player quantum PSM with perfect correctness. This leads to quadratic lower bounds for explicit functions. 2) The rank of the communication matrix of $f(x_1,x_2)$ lower bounds 2-player quantum PSM with perfect privacy but imperfect correctness. This implies a previously unknown lower bound on classical PSM with imperfect correctness. When allowing quantum communication and shared entanglement, these are the first lower bounds on quantum PSM that make use of the privacy condition. For upper bounds, we show: 1) Letting $s$ be the size of a quantum circuit computing $f$, $d_f$ be the circuit depth, $k$ the number of players, $n$ the number of bits received by each player, and $ε$ a correctness parameter, we obtain $\mathsf{PSM}_k^*(f) \leq (kn +s) \cdot \log^{O(d_f)}(s/ε)$. 2) The square of the Fourier 1 norm of $f$, $\Vert \hat{f}\Vert_1^2$, upper bounds the classical PSM complexity, $\mathsf{PSM}(f)\leq O(\Vert \hat{f} \Vert^2_1)$. In proving the first upper bound, we generalize existing $T$-depth based techniques for NLQC from $2$ to $k\geq 2$ parties, and consider cases where the Clifford layers are restricted to having small light cones.
one_liner: New lower and upper bounds clarify the cost of private simultaneous quantum message passing and its NLQC connections.
why_it_matters: The results sharpen complexity limits for privacy-preserving quantum communication, including bounds that explicitly use the privacy condition.
detailed_summary: The paper studies k-player private simultaneous message protocols where a referee learns only the target function value and no other input information. It proves lower bounds using Neciporuk's measure for perfect-correctness quantum PSM and communication-matrix rank for two-player PSM with perfect privacy and imperfect correctness. It also gives circuit-size and depth-dependent upper bounds for entanglement-assisted quantum PSM and a Fourier 1-norm upper bound for classical PSM.
main_result: The work establishes new privacy-sensitive lower bounds and constructive upper bounds for quantum and classical PSM models.
application_angle: These bounds inform the feasibility of secure multiparty quantum communication and non-local quantum computation protocols.

title: Block algebra for morphing circuits
abstract: Morphing circuits are a new paradigm for quantum error correction that relaxes hardware requirements. We present four constructions for CNOT-based CSS morphing circuits with explicit qubit connectivity degrees. All four constructions are specified in block algebra notation, with entries in algebras generated by permutation matrices. The first three are obtained by rewriting existing surface- and color-code morphing circuits; the fourth is a new three-round construction modeled on the 6.6.6 color code. The surface-code construction recovers the morphing circuit of Ref. [ST25] for two-block group algebra codes. Numerical search then instantiates these permutation matrices using regular representations of finite groups. [ST25] M. H. Shaw and B. M. Terhal, Phys. Rev. Lett. 134(9), 090602 (2025).
one_liner: Block-algebra constructions specify low-connectivity CSS morphing circuits for quantum error correction.
why_it_matters: Morphing circuits could lower hardware connectivity demands for fault-tolerant architectures while preserving structured CSS-code operations.
detailed_summary: The paper gives four CNOT-based CSS morphing-circuit constructions using block algebra with entries generated by permutation matrices. Three rewrite known surface- and color-code morphing circuits, while the fourth introduces a new three-round construction inspired by the 6.6.6 color code. Numerical searches instantiate the required permutations through regular representations of finite groups, making qubit connectivity degrees explicit.
main_result: The work formulates four explicit morphing-circuit families with controlled connectivity, including a new three-round color-code-like construction.
application_angle: The constructions target reduced hardware connectivity overhead for scalable fault-tolerant quantum processors.

title: Stable, bidirectional electro-optic transduction in thin film lithium tantalate
abstract: Efficient and stable microwave-optical transduction is a key enabling technology for distributed superconducting quantum computing and heterogeneous quantum networks. Electro-optic transducers based on thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) have shown strong promise, but demonstrations to date have been limited by various factors such as low frequency bias drift, low efficiency, fabrication complexity, and scalability. Here we demonstrate the first integrated electro-optic microwave-optical transducers realized in thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT), a material platform offering Pockels nonlinearity comparable to TFLN together with improved bias stability and high-power handling. We fabricate superconducting microwave resonators coupled to tunable photonic-molecule optical resonators using wafer-scale deep ultraviolet lithography, offering high-throughput production of hundreds of devices per wafer. Across six devices we observe coherent bidirectional conversion between C-band optical photons and 4.9-5.5 GHz microwave photons, with measured on-chip efficiencies and inferred single-photon coupling rates g_0/2π ~ 1 kHz consistent with theory. Continuous operation over multiple days is achieved using a static bias field with minimal feedback, demonstrating a major operational advantage. We further characterize optical loss statistics, microwave resonator performance, and optically induced added noise under pulsed pumping, finding less than one added photon for 100 microsecond pulses at the highest measured efficiencies. These results establish TFLT as a scalable and robust electro-optic platform for future quantum interconnects and modular quantum processors.
one_liner: Thin-film lithium tantalate devices coherently convert C-band optical photons and 5 GHz microwave photons for days.
why_it_matters: Stable bidirectional microwave-optical transduction is a key missing component for modular superconducting processors and quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate integrated electro-optic transducers in thin-film lithium tantalate, coupling superconducting microwave resonators to tunable photonic-molecule optical resonators. Six wafer-scale DUV-fabricated devices show coherent bidirectional conversion between C-band optics and 4.9-5.5 GHz microwaves, with inferred single-photon coupling rates near g0/2pi of 1 kHz. Continuous multi-day operation under a static bias field and less than one added photon for 100 microsecond pulsed operation highlight the platform's stability.
main_result: The paper demonstrates stable, bidirectional microwave-optical conversion in integrated TFLT devices with multi-day operation and low pulsed added noise.
application_angle: This directly supports quantum interconnects linking superconducting qubits to optical fiber networks.

title: Discrete phase symmetry of stationary states in bichromatically pumped Kerr microresonators
abstract: Bichromatically pumped Kerr microresonators exhibit phase bistability and multistability arising from four-wave mixing between pump and generated modes. Here we analyze the symmetry structure of stationary solutions in the coupled-mode description of such systems. We show that the two-pump coupled-mode equations are a particular case of a more general model with pumps placed at equally spaced modes. For this model, any stationary solution generates a finite family of stationary solutions through a discrete phase transformation. This transformation leaves the equations invariant and preserves the stability type of the corresponding stationary states. As a consequence, the possible stationary values of each individual mode form a regular polygon in the complex plane. The number of vertices is determined by the order of the mode index $μ$ in the group $\mathbb{Z}_n$, where $n$ is the separation between the pumped modes. Thus, the phase multistability structure depends not only on nonlinear dynamics but also on the arithmetic relation between the mode index and the pump separation. These results provide a simple symmetry-based explanation for the discrete phase structure observed in multimode Kerr resonators under bichromatic pumping.
one_liner: Bichromatically pumped Kerr microresonators have stationary states related by discrete phase symmetries.
why_it_matters: Understanding phase multistability helps control Kerr-comb and nonlinear microresonator states used in photonic signal processing.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes coupled-mode equations for Kerr microresonators pumped at two, or more generally equally spaced, modes. It shows that each stationary solution generates a finite family of stationary solutions under a discrete phase transformation that preserves stability. The allowed stationary values of each mode form regular polygons in the complex plane, with vertex count determined by the arithmetic relation between mode index and pump separation.
main_result: The main result is a symmetry rule predicting discrete phase families and stability-preserving multistability in multimode Kerr resonators.
application_angle: The result gives designers a compact way to predict and control phase states in nonlinear integrated photonic resonators.

title: Certifying Nonclassical Proper-Time Histories with a Quantum Clock
abstract: Quantum clocks can acquire relativistic phases from motional or gravitational proper-time differences, but reduced clock dephasing alone does not certify nonclassical proper-time histories. We formulate this distinction as a channel-certification problem. First, we show that any two-level single-time dephasing signal, including one generated by an effective quantum proper-time label, admits a classical random proper-time representation. We then define the convex set of classical mixtures of experimentally specified proper-time histories and prove a Choi-rank separation criterion for conditioned coherent history recombination. A two-branch Ramsey protocol gives explicit bright- and dark-port population witnesses outside this classical set. The certification is operational and relative to the specified history set: it rules out classical mixtures of the same implemented proper-time histories, not arbitrary classical protocols with different histories or controls.
one_liner: A quantum-clock Ramsey protocol certifies nonclassical proper-time histories beyond classical mixtures of implemented paths.
why_it_matters: It sharpens what quantum clocks can operationally prove about relativistic time histories in sensing and fundamental tests.
detailed_summary: The paper frames nonclassical proper-time histories as a channel-certification problem rather than relying on clock dephasing alone. It proves that any two-level single-time dephasing signal admits a classical random proper-time model, then derives a Choi-rank separation criterion for conditioned coherent history recombination. A two-branch Ramsey protocol supplies bright- and dark-port population witnesses outside the specified classical history set.
main_result: The work provides operational witnesses that rule out classical mixtures of the same implemented proper-time histories.
application_angle: The protocol informs precision clock experiments seeking certifiable quantum-relativistic sensing signatures.

title: Matrix phase-space representations for quantum symmetries
abstract: We introduce a general phase-space representation that includes global quantum symmetries in the basis expansion. This method, called matrix phase-space, projects the basis onto a reduced Hilbert space, which can greatly reduce sampling errors of many-body quantum simulations and unifies several previous phase-space methods. The purpose of this paper is to provide detailed proofs of basic theorems and operator identities. We also treat several different types of symmetries. To illustrate the benefits of matrix phase-space methods, we give a detailed derivation of a recent application to the topical problem of verifying the outputs of Gaussian boson sampling (GBS) quantum computers with photon number resolving detectors. This has exponential complexity, and using parity symmetry reduces sampling errors by very large factors relative to earlier methods.
one_liner: Matrix phase-space representations exploit symmetries to reduce sampling error in many-body quantum simulation.
why_it_matters: Symmetry-aware verification can make classically checking photonic quantum-computing outputs more tractable.
detailed_summary: The paper develops matrix phase-space representations that incorporate global quantum symmetries directly into the basis expansion. By projecting onto reduced Hilbert spaces, the method unifies earlier phase-space approaches and can strongly reduce sampling errors. A detailed application to Gaussian boson sampling verification shows how parity symmetry improves estimates for photon-number-resolving detector outputs with exponential complexity.
main_result: The authors prove the formal identities behind matrix phase-space methods and show large sampling-error reductions for GBS verification using parity symmetry.
application_angle: This helps benchmark and verify photonic quantum hardware where direct classical simulation is prohibitively expensive.

title: Explicit Quantum Circuit Simulation of Nonlinear 1-Dimensional Fluid with Carleman-linearized Boltzmann Method
abstract: Quantum computation of fluid dynamics has attracted growing attention as a key application of fault-tolerant quantum computers anticipated in the coming decade, with lattice Boltzmann methods emerging as a particularly promising approach. Explicit and efficient elementary-gate-level circuit simulations, however, have so far been demonstrated only in the linear case. Here we include the leading nonlinearity through second-order Carleman linearization of the one-dimensional Boltzmann equation, and demonstrate, via explicit quantum-circuit simulation, the preparation of the final-time state using a Taylor-expansion-based ODE solver based on the quantum singular value transformation. With this construction, we analyze the gate and qubit complexities, which scale logarithmically with the grid size, the nonlinearity captured by the higher-order Carleman linearization, and the practical utility of higher-order expansions in the Taylor ODE solver. The construction provides a concrete baseline for computational cost reduction and further developments such as extensions to higher dimensions, complex geometries, and the extraction of physical quantities, towards industrially useful quantum CFD.
one_liner: Gate-level circuits simulate nonlinear 1D Boltzmann fluid dynamics using Carleman linearization and QSVT ODE solving.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete resource baseline for future fault-tolerant quantum algorithms for industrial fluid simulation.
detailed_summary: The paper extends explicit gate-level quantum circuit simulation of lattice Boltzmann dynamics beyond the linear case by adding leading nonlinearity through second-order Carleman linearization. It prepares the final-time state using a Taylor-expansion ODE solver based on quantum singular value transformation. The analysis reports gate and qubit complexities that scale logarithmically with grid size and examines the utility of higher-order Taylor expansions.
platform_or_system: fault-tolerant quantum circuit for 1D Boltzmann fluids
main_result: The main result is an explicit elementary-gate circuit construction for nonlinear 1D Boltzmann dynamics with analyzed qubit and gate costs.
application_angle: The work is a step toward practical quantum acceleration for computational fluid dynamics in engineering workflows.

title: Quantum Network Routing based on Surface Code Error Correction
abstract: Quantum networks encounter unavoidable channel noises and erasure errors, presenting a huge obstacle in designing protocols that attain both high reliability and efficiency. Typically, quantum networks fall into two categories: those utilize quantum entanglements for quantum teleportation, and those directly transfer the actual quantum messages. In this paper, we present SurfNet, a quantum network that inherits the main advantages from both categories. It employs surface codes as logical qubits for encoding messages, and utilizes two parallel communication channels to fault-tolerantly transfer each surface code in a modular manner. Our approach of using surface codes can timely correct both operational and photon loss errors within the network, and the integration of the two channels within the network can greatly improve network throughput. For the implementation of SurfNet, we propose a novel network architecture, designed to better integrate surface codes into quantum networks. We also propose a novel error correction decoder, designed to fully utilize the modular characteristic of surface codes within our network. Simulation results demonstrate that SurfNet with its decoder significantly enhances the communication fidelity within quantum networks.
one_liner: SurfNet encodes quantum-network messages as surface-code logical qubits and routes them over dual channels for fault-tolerant delivery.
why_it_matters: It targets the core bottleneck in quantum networking: preserving message fidelity under both operational noise and photon loss while maintaining throughput.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes SurfNet, a network architecture that directly transfers quantum messages encoded as surface-code logical qubits. Each surface-code block is sent modularly over two parallel communication channels, allowing timely correction of operational errors and photon-loss erasures. A custom decoder exploits the modular surface-code structure, and simulations report substantially improved communication fidelity.
main_result: SurfNet demonstrates simulated high-fidelity quantum-message transfer by combining surface-code encoding, dual-channel transport, and a modular decoder.
application_angle: The approach is relevant to reliable quantum internet links where both loss tolerance and network throughput are practical deployment constraints.

title: Accidental Symmetry in the Tavis-Cummings Model via the Schwinger Boson Representation
abstract: The Jaynes-Cummings (JC) Hamiltonian is a paradigmatic model of light-matter interaction and, more generally, qubit-boson interactions, widely used across atomic, optical, and superconducting qubit platforms. In the multi-qubit setting, where n qubits are identically coupled to a single boson mode, this interaction is known as the Tavis-Cummings (TC) Hamiltonian. The structure of the TC model is usually understood in terms of two standard symmetries: permutation invariance of the qubits and a U(1) symmetry associated with conservation of the total excitation number. Here we identify an additional, independent "accidental" symmetry of the TC Hamiltonian and construct the corresponding conserved observable. We show that, for n>2 qubits, this symmetry imposes strong constraints on the realizable unitary transformations. These constraints persist in the presence of the global $J_z$ Hamiltonian, but are removed by adding $J_z^2$, even though $J_z^2$ preserves both permutation invariance and the U(1) symmetry. Finally, we explain the origin of this previously unnoticed symmetry using Schwinger's boson representation of angular momentum. These restrictions have important implications for controllability of the TC system and for its applications to quantum computing, which are investigated further in a companion paper.
one_liner: A previously unnoticed conserved symmetry in the Tavis-Cummings model constrains multi-qubit controllability for n>2.
why_it_matters: The result identifies a hidden control limitation in a central light-matter model used across cavity, atomic, and superconducting-qubit hardware.
detailed_summary: The work finds an independent accidental symmetry of the multi-qubit Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian beyond permutation symmetry and excitation-number conservation. Using the Schwinger boson representation, it constructs the conserved observable and shows that for more than two qubits the symmetry restricts reachable unitaries. Adding global Jz does not remove the restriction, while adding Jz^2 does.
main_result: The paper derives a hidden conserved quantity that limits controllability in the Tavis-Cummings model for n>2 qubits.
application_angle: This matters for designing quantum-computing control protocols in platforms where many qubits couple collectively to a shared bosonic mode.

title: Graph Reinforcement Learning for Calibration-Aware Quantum Circuit Routing
abstract: Quantum circuit routing is a key step in compiling programs for noisy intermediate-scale quantum processors. Routes that appear efficient by standard overhead metrics can still lose fidelity when they pass through poorly calibrated couplers. We study a calibration-aware graph reinforcement-learning router that uses same-day IBM Heron r2 calibration data to choose hardware-edge SWAPs. We train the policy with proximal policy optimization and evaluate it with exact simulated fidelity across nine Munich Quantum Toolkit (MQT) Bench circuits and three calibration snapshots. Across these evaluations, pooled mean exact fidelity is $0.727$, compared with $0.440$ for SABRE-best20 and $0.481$ for target-aware SABRE. We observed that fidelity gains came with higher routed two-qubit counts and were concentrated in 5 qubit and 8 qubit circuit families; under the fixed tree action graph, all 10 qubit families favored SABRE-best20. Overall, our results show that calibration-aware learned routing can improve fidelity beyond gate-count-driven compilation.
one_liner: A graph reinforcement-learning router uses live IBM Heron calibration data to choose SWAPs that improve circuit fidelity.
why_it_matters: It shows that calibration-aware routing can outperform standard gate-count-driven compilation on realistic noisy superconducting hardware maps.
detailed_summary: The paper trains a graph RL policy with proximal policy optimization to route quantum circuits using same-day IBM Heron r2 calibration data. It evaluates exact simulated fidelity on nine MQT Bench circuits across three calibration snapshots. The learned router achieves pooled mean exact fidelity of 0.727 versus 0.440 for SABRE-best20 and 0.481 for target-aware SABRE, though gains depend on circuit size and action-graph constraints.
main_result: The calibration-aware RL router improves pooled simulated circuit fidelity over SABRE baselines while sometimes accepting more two-qubit gates.
application_angle: It points toward compilers that adapt to daily hardware calibration instead of optimizing only abstract connectivity or gate count.

title: Quantum walk-based optimisation for capacitated vehicle routing with homogeneous and heterogeneous fleets
abstract: The capacitated vehicle routing problem (CVRP) is an appealing candidate for quantum optimisation due to its combinatorial complexity and practical importance. However, the problem's constrained search space poses a challenge for such quantum algorithms. We introduce a quantum walk-based optimisation algorithm (QWOA) for the CVRP with homogeneous or heterogeneous vehicle fleets, addressing this challenge through a continuous-time quantum walk over a product space that coincides with combinatorial structures intrinsic to the CVRP solution space. Relative to the prior QWOA-based formulation, this approach reduces the per-layer gate complexity from $\mathcal{O}(n^{3}\log n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n^{2}\log n)$ and supports a circuit parameterisation schedule generated by a fixed number of classical parameters. Exact state-vector simulation on instances with up to $n=8$ customers and $K=3$ vehicles demonstrates improved convergence to low-cost solutions using markedly fewer objective function evaluations, with the advantage broadening as problem size increases. These results identify structured product-space walks as a promising tool for optimisation over constrained combinatorial spaces.
one_liner: A structured quantum-walk optimizer tackles capacitated vehicle routing with lower per-layer gate complexity and fewer evaluations.
why_it_matters: It advances constrained quantum optimization for a logistics problem with direct industrial relevance and difficult combinatorial structure.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a quantum walk-based optimization algorithm for capacitated vehicle routing with homogeneous or heterogeneous fleets. Its continuous-time walk runs over a product space aligned with CVRP solution structure, reducing per-layer gate complexity from O(n^3 log n) to O(n^2 log n). State-vector simulations up to eight customers and three vehicles show faster convergence to low-cost solutions with fewer objective evaluations.
main_result: The algorithm reduces QWOA layer complexity and improves simulated convergence for small CVRP instances.
application_angle: The method is application-relevant for quantum approaches to routing and logistics optimization under hard capacity constraints.

title: QuBE/Qubex: an integrated hardware-software system for superconducting qubit experiments with broadband control
abstract: Achieving high-fidelity operation in large-scale superconducting qubit systems requires not only control hardware with broad frequency coverage, low crosstalk, and tight synchronization but also software that coordinates system configuration, experiment execution, and data analysis. Here we present an integrated qubit-control system that combines broadband microwave hardware with a pulse-level software stack for scalable superconducting qubit experiments. The hardware provides broadband microwave coverage, including an instantaneous span of up to 1.6 GHz from a control output, while the software reduces setup and calibration overhead through automated configuration and built-in experiment workflows. We validate the system on a 64-qubit fixed-frequency transmon chip through full-chip frequency identification and representative demonstrations, including multi-unit far-detuned cross-resonance calibration and benchmarking that yields a measured two-qubit gate fidelity of 98.34%, and multilevel readout beyond the computational subspace. By disclosing the hardware architecture and releasing the software stack as open source, this work provides an inspectable hardware-software foundation for scalable superconducting qubit control experiments.
one_liner: QuBE/Qubex combines broadband microwave control hardware and open software workflows for superconducting-qubit experiments.
why_it_matters: Integrated, inspectable control stacks are essential for scaling superconducting processors from device tests to reproducible multi-qubit operation.
detailed_summary: The paper presents an integrated hardware-software stack for superconducting qubit experiments, pairing broadband microwave electronics with pulse-level experiment software. The hardware offers up to 1.6 GHz instantaneous span from a control output, while the software automates configuration, calibration, execution, and analysis workflows. Validation on a 64-qubit fixed-frequency transmon chip includes full-chip frequency identification, far-detuned cross-resonance calibration, 98.34% measured two-qubit gate fidelity, and multilevel readout.
main_result: QuBE/Qubex demonstrates broadband scalable control and workflow automation on a 64-qubit superconducting-qubit device.
application_angle: The open hardware architecture and software stack can lower setup friction for labs building and benchmarking larger superconducting systems.

title: Simple analytical flux-tuned iSWAP pulses for leakage suppression
abstract: Fast, high-fidelity two-qubit gates are a key requirement for fault-tolerant quantum computation. Tunable coupler architectures provide a flexible approach for implementing entangling gates through flux control with large on-off ratios, but fast flux modulation can induce diabatic transitions and population leakage to non-computational states, limiting gate performance. Here we present an analytical flux control method enabling derivative removal by adiabatic gate ($Φ$-DRAG) for suppressing leakage in flux tunable two-qubit gates.
