We show that $Φ$-DRAG differs fundamentally from conventional microwave implementations and derive modified flux modulation protocols that suppress leakage below $10^{-4}$ for fast entangling gates. The method remains effective across a range of asymmetry between qubit anharmonicities and different circuit parameters, enabling high-fidelity two-qubit gates within the fifteen nanosecond range.
one_liner: Analytical flux-tuned iSWAP pulses apply Phi-DRAG control to suppress leakage in fast tunable-coupler gates.
why_it_matters: Leakage from fast flux modulation is a key obstacle to high-fidelity two-qubit gates in tunable superconducting architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper derives an analytical flux-control method for derivative removal by adiabatic gate, tailored to flux-tunable two-qubit gates. It shows that Phi-DRAG differs from microwave DRAG and produces modified flux modulation protocols that suppress leakage below 1e-4. The approach remains robust across qubit-anharmonicity asymmetry and circuit-parameter variation, supporting entangling gates around 15 ns.
main_result: Phi-DRAG flux pulses suppress non-computational leakage below 1e-4 while enabling fast iSWAP-family entangling gates.
application_angle: The technique is directly useful for improving gate speed and fidelity in tunable-coupler superconducting quantum processors.

title: Hamiltonian-Aware ADAPT Variational Quantum Eigensolver for Molecular Ground-State Simulation
abstract: Designing compact ansätze in Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) is crucial for solving energetic problems of practical molecules on near-term quantum devices. However, existing Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter (ADAPT) ansätze face two challenges: improper operator selection and accumulation of degraded operators. In this paper, we propose the Hamiltonian-Aware (HA) ADAPT-VQE algorithm to address these issues. First, we establish a novel excitation operator selection criterion. It breaks the local constraint of existing criteria by incorporating Hamiltonian information, prioritizes physically meaningful excitation operators, and incurs no extra classical or quantum computational overhead. Furthermore, we develop a problem-adaptive method for discriminating and pruning redundant excitation operators stemming from improper selection and inevitable degradation. This method balances redundant operator pruning and convergence guarantee, and is applicable to ansätze with arbitrary scales. Systematic numerical experiments on typical strongly correlated molecular systems demonstrate that our HA-ADAPT-VQE avoids energy plateaus and outperforms baseline algorithms in terms of energy error, ansatz size, and measurement cost. This work offers an efficient, robust ansatz construction paradigm, facilitating the development and practical deployment of large-scale VQE in quantum chemistry.
one_liner: Hamiltonian-aware ADAPT-VQE selects and prunes excitation operators to build smaller, more accurate molecular ansatze.
why_it_matters: More compact, measurement-efficient VQE circuits are directly relevant to making quantum chemistry useful on near-term quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces HA-ADAPT-VQE, which uses Hamiltonian information to prioritize physically meaningful excitation operators without extra quantum or classical overhead. It also adds a problem-adaptive pruning method to remove redundant or degraded operators while preserving convergence. Numerical tests on strongly correlated molecular systems show reduced energy error, smaller ansatze, and lower measurement cost versus baseline ADAPT methods.
main_result: HA-ADAPT-VQE avoids energy plateaus and improves energy accuracy, ansatz size, and measurement cost in molecular ground-state simulations.
application_angle: The method targets practical quantum chemistry workloads where circuit depth and measurement budgets limit deployment on real devices.

title: Achieving Heisenberg limit under noisy conditions with quantum Zeno dynamics and dynamical decoupling
abstract: Quantum Zeno dynamics (QZD) and dynamical decoupling (DD) are useful tools that enable the effective suppression of noise in quantum systems. We consider the problem of when (i) noise can be suppressed and (ii) Heisenberg limit (HL) can be achieved in quantum metrology, and prove necessary and sufficient conditions for when QZD and DD are useful for achieving these two goals. We also show that in the Markovian regime, there are scenarios where preventing errors using QZD/DD may enable HL to be achieved where current QEC methods may not. Finally, we demonstrate that the combination of both techniques can allow individually imperfect QZD and DD strategies to saturate HL.
one_liner: Quantum Zeno dynamics and dynamical decoupling are characterized for reaching Heisenberg-limited metrology under noise.
why_it_matters: It gives operational criteria for when control-based noise suppression can preserve quantum-enhanced sensing without full error correction.
detailed_summary: The work derives necessary and sufficient conditions under which QZD and DD can suppress noise and allow Heisenberg-limit scaling in quantum metrology. It identifies Markovian scenarios where QZD/DD can enable Heisenberg scaling even when current QEC approaches may not. The authors also show that combining imperfect QZD and DD protocols can still saturate the Heisenberg limit.
main_result: The paper proves when QZD and DD can suppress sensing noise and restore Heisenberg-limited scaling.
application_angle: These criteria can guide robust sensor-control design in platforms where active error correction is too costly or impractical.

title: Coupling-Grouped XY-QAOA for Joint Anomaly-Feature Selection
abstract: Selecting anomalous samples and explanatory features under fixed budgets defines a coupled constrained-optimization problem. Sequential feature-first selection ranks features before choosing samples, which can overlook features whose utility depends on which samples are selected, especially when scores are calibrated from reference data that may be limited, noisy, or drifting. We instead formulate the task as joint sample-feature selection under the same fixed counts. In the analyzed formal model, calibration-error sensitivity grows linearly with the number of samples for feature-first ordering but stays constant for joint selection. We introduce Coupling-Grouped XY-QAOA, a constraint-preserving grouped-angle variant for the resulting optimization problem. On matched sparse IBM Heron R3 benchmarks, a hardware-aware implementation reduces circuit depth by 45.9%-61.3% and two-qubit gates by 2.6%-5.2% relative to Qiskit optimization level 3 on the CZ-basis target. It enables, to our knowledge, the largest reported width-depth configurations for constraint-preserving bipartite-selection QAOA hardware executions with feasible-sector retention: 64 qubits at p=2 and 36 qubits at p=3. The 20-qubit p=5 runs retain 63% valid samples. Across 36-64 qubits, fixed-angle runs yield lower-energy feasible samples than matched random-feasible sampling. Warm starts reduce the gap to strict-feasible classical references by 57.5%-80.5%, and near-budget repair matches the sparse classical reference at 36 qubits. Benchmarks show gains in balanced fixed-budget regimes, and noiseless simulations show that problem-structured angle grouping improves over same-depth XY-QAOA and matched-parameter, type-preserving randomization controls. Overall, the results support calibrated joint selection and hardware-realizable constrained-mixer execution in the tested regimes.
one_liner: Coupling-Grouped XY-QAOA executes constrained joint anomaly-feature selection on IBM Heron R3 up to 64 qubits.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates hardware-aware constrained QAOA at unusually large width-depth settings for a practical data-selection optimization task.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates joint anomalous-sample and feature selection as a coupled fixed-budget optimization problem and implements it with a constraint-preserving grouped-angle XY-QAOA. On sparse IBM Heron R3 benchmarks, the hardware-aware circuits reduce depth by 45.9%-61.3% relative to Qiskit optimization level 3 and run feasible-sector experiments up to 64 qubits at p=2 and 36 qubits at p=3. Fixed-angle and warm-start runs improve feasible-sample energy versus random-feasible and reduce gaps to strict-feasible classical references.
main_result: The method runs large constrained bipartite-selection QAOA instances with feasible-sector retention and improved solution quality over matched baselines.
application_angle: The work connects quantum optimization hardware to calibrated anomaly and feature selection workflows where joint budget constraints matter.

title: Experimental Design Space Exploration of Ultra-Low Threshold Hybrid III-V/Si Quantum Dot Microring Lasers
abstract: In this work, we report on the design strategies and experimental validation of ultra-low threshold ($< 0.8\,\mathrm{mA}$) hybrid III--V/Si quantum dot (InAs/GaAs) micro-ring lasers with optical output powers $> 2\,\mathrm{mW}$ for $1.3\,μ\mathrm{m}$ emission. The multi-dimensional design exploration allows for the demonstration of record wall-plug efficiencies ($\sim 10\%$) and threshold current densities ($109\,\mathrm{A/cm^2}$) for these compact sources on silicon. We also demonstrate the thermal performance of several designs with record characteristic temperature values of $T_0 = 212\,\mathrm{K}$, indicating minimal temperature dependence of the threshold current. In addition, the high differential gain allows for the demonstration of 3-dB bandwidths up to $5\,\mathrm{GHz}$.
one_liner: Hybrid III-V/Si quantum-dot microring lasers reach sub-0.8 mA thresholds, >2 mW output, and about 10% wall-plug efficiency.
why_it_matters: Efficient 1.3 um lasers integrated on silicon are key building blocks for scalable photonic links and chip-scale optical systems.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally explore a multidimensional design space for hybrid InAs/GaAs quantum-dot microring lasers bonded to silicon. They validate devices with threshold currents below 0.8 mA, output powers above 2 mW, threshold current density of 109 A/cm2, and wall-plug efficiency near 10%. Thermal testing reports characteristic temperatures up to T0 = 212 K, and modulation measurements show 3-dB bandwidths up to 5 GHz.
main_result: The devices demonstrate ultra-low threshold lasing on silicon with record efficiency, strong thermal stability, and GHz-class modulation.
application_angle: These compact sources could support energy-efficient optical I/O, datacenter photonics, and dense silicon photonic transceiver integration.

title: Driven-dissipative entanglement of distant giant atoms
abstract: Quantum interconnects distribute entanglement via controlled light-matter interactions for quantum computing and sensing applications. Many entanglement generation schemes use coherent, reversible interactions that require precisely calibrated pulses to execute. In contrast, driven-dissipative protocols use a continuous-wave drive in the presence of correlated dissipation to stabilize entanglement in protected (dark) states. However, the same dissipation that generates the entanglement also limits its utility once the stabilization protocol ends. Here, we engineer a superconducting system of two giant artificial atoms coupled sequentially to a waveguide, with tunable individual and correlated dissipation enabled by interference between coupling points. Continuously driving the atoms through the waveguide exploits correlated dissipation to generate remote entanglement. We then tune the qubit frequencies in situ to suppress individual dissipation and thereby preserve the entanglement, achieving a Bell-state fidelity F = 0.89 +/- 0.02. This demonstration indicates that the driven dissipation of giant atoms is a viable approach for distributing entanglement across quantum networks.
one_liner: Two distant superconducting giant atoms use driven dissipation to generate and preserve waveguide-mediated Bell entanglement.
why_it_matters: It shows a route to continuous-wave entanglement distribution that can be stabilized, then protected, in a superconducting quantum network element.
detailed_summary: The experiment engineers two giant artificial atoms sequentially coupled to a waveguide with tunable individual and correlated dissipation from interference between coupling points. A continuous drive through the waveguide uses correlated dissipation to stabilize remote entanglement in a dark state. The qubit frequencies are then tuned in situ to suppress individual dissipation and preserve the state, yielding Bell-state fidelity F = 0.89 +/- 0.02.
platform_or_system: superconducting giant atoms in waveguide QED
main_result: The system generates remote driven-dissipative entanglement and preserves it with measured Bell-state fidelity of 0.89 +/- 0.02.
application_angle: The protocol is relevant to quantum interconnects that distribute entanglement across superconducting processors or sensing nodes.

title: Representation-Induced Symmetry Trapping in Adaptive Variational Quantum Simulations of Multi-Reference Topologies
abstract: Evaluating the trainability of adaptive quantum chemistry algorithms under multi-reference static correlation requires understanding how representation topologies intertwine with molecular geometry. We systematically expose a deep physical dependence on point-group symmetry by evaluating a spin-conserved SUSD operator pool across highly stretched configurations (2 x Re) of asymmetric LiH, symmetric BeH2, and asymmetric H2O. Under asymmetric distortions, the non-local mapping constraints of the Bravyi-Kitaev transformation create an optimization trapping effect--an encodement-locked manifestation of the broader barren plateau crisis. Crucially, by comparing these to the symmetrical stretching baseline of BeH2, we demonstrate that the preservation of point-group symmetry structurally protects the optimization landscape, proving that ansatz symmetry restrictions are necessary but insufficient without accounting for the underlying fermion-to-qubit representation. While current methods rely on numerical pruning to throttle pool sizes, our structural approach establishes that the mapping representation remains a critical factor in maintaining landscape trainability. Furthermore, exploiting structural overlap within our pool, we introduce a covariance-driven, adaptive shot-allocation filter. Diverging from static energy-variance minimization frameworks, our allocation engine operates as a dynamic runtime diagnostic tool. By continuously monitoring the gradient precision threshold epsilon, it aggressively prunes dead symmetry channels and triggers an automated circuit-termination sequence upon detecting representation-induced flat-lined states (dE/dtheta approx 0). This integration of algebraic measurement reuse with topology-aware statistical filtering provides a promising, resource-efficient strategy for executing deep variational algorithms on early fault-tolerant architectures.
one_liner: Adaptive VQE simulations reveal representation-induced symmetry trapping and add covariance-driven shot allocation diagnostics.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete trainability failure mode for quantum chemistry algorithms and proposes runtime tools to detect and stop wasted circuits.
detailed_summary: The paper studies spin-conserved SUSD operator pools for stretched LiH, BeH2, and H2O to show how molecular symmetry and fermion-to-qubit mapping shape adaptive VQE landscapes. It finds that Bravyi-Kitaev non-local mapping constraints can induce optimization trapping under asymmetric distortions, while preserved point-group symmetry protects trainability. A covariance-driven adaptive shot-allocation filter monitors gradient precision, prunes inactive symmetry channels, and terminates circuits when flat-lined states are detected.
main_result: The study demonstrates representation-induced symmetry trapping and proposes a dynamic shot-allocation and termination filter for adaptive VQE.
application_angle: The diagnostics could reduce wasted measurements and improve reliability for deep variational chemistry workloads on early fault-tolerant hardware.

title: Foundations of Practical Quantum Advantage in Quantum-Informed Machine Learning for Predicting Chaos
abstract: We develop theoretical foundations for a practical quantum-advantage mechanism in quantum-informed machine learning for chaotic dynamical systems. A family of k-indexed higher-order quantum statistical priors (Q-Priors) hosts the k-point marginal of the invariant measure on n_q = kq qubits, extending the single-site construction of prior work. We prove a two-stage advantage. In the representation stage, superposition and entanglement compactly store non-factorisable spatial correlations of the invariant measure on n_q qubits. In the extraction stage, joint Bell measurements on two copies estimate any post hoc Pauli functional with a copy-pair count independent of n_q, whereas any adaptive single-copy protocol for the corresponding full-Pauli read-out requires Omega(2^(n_q)) copies; this is a provable quantum-classical separation in copy-measurement complexity. The two-copy read-out is realised in simulation and on IQM superconducting processors. Two case studies instantiate the mechanism in workflows of independent scientific value: a turbulent channel-flow study in which the two-copy read-out yields a named non-diagonal correlator of the invariant measure (the velocity-direction coherence), and a medium-range weather forecasting workflow on the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts ERA5 reanalysis in which the diagonal k <= 2 Q-Prior steers a Koopman rollout, improves anomaly-correlation skill by 10-39% across 48-240 h lead times, and reduces the long-horizon collapse of rollouts onto a static mean field. The two conditions of our practical-advantage definition are met at complementary levels, identifying a candidate route to practical quantum advantage before fault-tolerant hardware.
one_liner: Quantum-informed priors use two-copy Bell readout to extract chaotic-system correlations and improve medium-range weather rollouts.
why_it_matters: It offers an experimentally tested route to practical quantum advantage for scientific ML before fault-tolerant machines are available.
detailed_summary: The work defines higher-order Q-Priors that encode k-point marginals of invariant measures on kq qubits, using entanglement to represent non-factorizable correlations. A two-copy Bell-measurement protocol estimates post hoc Pauli functionals with copy complexity independent of the register size, contrasting with exponential-copy single-copy readout. The authors simulate the method and run it on IQM superconducting processors, then apply it to turbulent flow correlators and ERA5-based Koopman weather rollouts.
main_result: The paper demonstrates two-copy extraction of Pauli functionals and reports 10-39% anomaly-correlation skill gains across 48-240 h weather forecast lead times.
application_angle: The approach targets practical quantum-enhanced scientific prediction workflows in turbulence, weather, and other chaotic systems.

title: Polarization-controlled transport of light in a two-dimensional waveguide
abstract: Light propagation in disordered media is particularly complex due to the interference effects and polarization-mixing terms. Two-dimensional systems are particularly interesting because they feature two decoupled scattering channels, with distinct spectral statistics due to the presence of polarization-coupling terms in only one of them. We here show that in one channel, Anderson localization bounds the light transport both longitudinally and transversely. In the second channel, in contrast, diffusive transport takes place independently of scatterer density. Our results open new possibilities for engineering of light transport in two-dimensional waveguides.
one_liner: A 2D disordered waveguide shows polarization-dependent light transport with one localized channel and one robustly diffusive channel.
why_it_matters: Polarization-selective localization and diffusion could become a design knob for routing and filtering light in integrated disordered photonics.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes light propagation in a two-dimensional disordered waveguide where polarization structure separates transport into two scattering channels. One channel exhibits Anderson localization that confines light longitudinally and transversely. The other remains diffusive regardless of scatterer density, attributed to the different role of polarization-coupling terms.
main_result: The main result is polarization-controlled switching between bounded Anderson-localized transport and density-independent diffusive transport.
application_angle: This provides a route to engineer waveguide transport, confinement, and disorder-tolerant optical pathways through polarization control.

title: Phonon polariton confinement in isotopically pure MOVPE-grown BN triangles
abstract: Phonon polaritons, quasiparticles formed by the resonant hybridization of light and lattice vibrations, exhibit unique properties like the possibility of hyperbolic dispersions. In this context, exfoliated hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) has emerged as a promising material for phonon polariton-based research. However, to advance toward practical applications, it is essential to demonstrate efficient phonon-polariton propagation and confinement in large-area epitaxial BN. To address this topic, we use metalorganic vapor phase epitaxy (MOVPE)-grown BN and investigate the phonon polariton properties using scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) and nanoscale Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (nano-FTIR). We report remarkably long phonon polariton propagation lengths, indicating the high crystalline quality of the BN layer. By using epitaxially grown isotopically pure triangular islands, we further demonstrate efficient phonon-polariton confinement with mode patterns tunable by the incident light wavelength. Our results pave the way for implementing all-epitaxial, microscale, high-quality polariton resonators for nanophotonics and quantum optics.
one_liner: Isotopically pure MOVPE-grown BN triangles confine long-lived phonon polaritons with wavelength-tunable mode patterns.
why_it_matters: Epitaxial BN polariton resonators move nanophotonic and quantum-optics devices closer to scalable wafer-compatible fabrication.
detailed_summary: The authors study phonon polaritons in MOVPE-grown boron nitride using scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy and nanoscale FTIR. They observe long propagation lengths, indicating high crystalline quality in the epitaxial BN layer. In isotopically pure triangular islands, the polaritons are efficiently confined and show mode patterns controlled by the incident wavelength.
platform_or_system: isotopically pure epitaxial BN triangular islands
main_result: The paper demonstrates long-range phonon-polariton propagation and wavelength-tunable confinement in MOVPE-grown BN microstructures.
application_angle: The result supports scalable all-epitaxial polariton resonators for infrared nanophotonics, sensing, and quantum-optical interfaces.

title: Observation of Non-Gaussian Magnon Dynamics in a Two-Dimensional Long-Range XY Model
abstract: Non-Gaussian evolution of high-order spin correlations characterizes important properties of quantum many-body systems. In practice, decoherence, statistical fluctuation and miscalibration of experimental parameters all hinder the witness of non-Gaussian dynamics. Here we demonstrate the crossover between Gaussian and non-Gaussian dynamics on a two-dimensional XY model with long-range and spatially structured interaction using a trapped ion quantum simulator. We prepare different initial densities of magnon excitations and verify the dynamics of single-spin observables for the engineered Hamiltonian. Then we compare the high-order spin correlations with the mean-field solution and the Holstein-Primakoff approximation, and demonstrate the non-Gaussian behavior in a way independent of the calibration errors. Our work provides a verifiable path from classically simulatable dynamics to regimes where quantum advantage may emerge.
one_liner: A trapped-ion simulator observes calibration-robust non-Gaussian magnon dynamics in a 2D long-range XY model.
why_it_matters: Non-Gaussian spin correlations mark movement beyond mean-field and Gaussian regimes toward harder many-body dynamics on programmable hardware.
detailed_summary: The experiment engineers a two-dimensional long-range XY Hamiltonian with spatially structured interactions in a trapped-ion quantum simulator. By preparing different magnon densities, the authors verify single-spin dynamics and compare higher-order spin correlations against mean-field and Holstein-Primakoff approximations. They identify a crossover from Gaussian to non-Gaussian dynamics using witnesses designed to be insensitive to calibration errors.
main_result: The main result is an experimental observation of non-Gaussian magnon dynamics in a 2D long-range XY spin model.
application_angle: The work benchmarks programmable quantum simulators in regimes relevant to many-body physics and possible quantum advantage.

title: Quantum Logic Codes: Complete Transversal Logical Clifford Instruction Sets for High-Rate Stabilizer Quantum Error Correcting Codes
abstract: We study the structure and transversal logical capabilities of stabilizer quantum error correcting codes. Among our results, we identify universal lower bounds on circuit depth to generate a full logical Clifford algebra, and develop novel constructions of logical transversal gates including a new depth-one transversal phase $\mathrm{\overline{S}}$ gate in the rotated surface code and a depth-one intra-block $\mathrm{\overline{CZ}}$ gate in the 2D-toric code that generalizes to all odd distances and all lengths $L\ge3$, respectively. Finally, we construct a high-rate non-LDPC CSS code family with parameters $[[n,\sqrt{n},Θ({n^β})]]$ where $β\approx 0.2823$ in one demonstrated case, that provably possesses a constant-depth complete 2-local transversal logical Clifford basis instruction set architecture (ISA) composed of all individually targeted $\mathrm{\overline{S}}$, $\mathrm{\overline{SHS}} = \sqrt{X}$, and $\mathrm{\overline{CZ}}$ gates. This ISA is depth-one for certain subfamilies that we design and generally constant-depth under certain conditions. The code family is built from a small code with parameters $[[n_0, 2, d_0]]$, and is tunable in the standard way: it tiles out to form utility-scale logical qubit counts, and it scales up through concatenation to achieve higher distances and error suppression. We show that this construction preserves the depth-one complete transversal logical Clifford basis ISA when composed with these commuting construction actions, inheriting structure from the core codes so that at scale the complete logical Clifford basis ISA remains depth-one up to depth-two addressable operations between tiled cores. We call these Quantum Logic Codes.
one_liner: Quantum Logic Codes provide high-rate stabilizer-code constructions with complete constant-depth transversal logical Clifford instruction sets.
why_it_matters: Fast transversal logical Clifford gates could reduce overhead and latency in scalable fault-tolerant quantum computing architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper studies transversal logical capabilities of stabilizer quantum error-correcting codes and proves lower bounds on circuit depth for generating full logical Clifford algebras. It introduces depth-one logical phase gates for rotated surface codes and depth-one intra-block logical CZ gates for 2D toric codes. It then constructs high-rate non-LDPC CSS Quantum Logic Codes with constant-depth, and in some subfamilies depth-one, complete 2-local transversal logical Clifford bases.
main_result: The main result is a family of Quantum Logic Codes supporting complete constant-depth transversal logical Clifford instruction sets at scalable logical qubit counts.
application_angle: The construction is relevant to fault-tolerant processor design where logical gate parallelism and low-depth control are hardware bottlenecks.

title: Approximate quantum error correction theory of non-isometric codes
abstract: Non-isometric encoding arises in various important contexts in quantum error correction, most notably in the finite-energy, non-ideal codewords inevitable in experimental realizations of continuous-variable codes, and holographic quantum gravity. In this work, we present a general and systematic theory of non-isometric quantum error-correcting codes. In particular, we employ the approximate quantum error correction framework to quantitatively study the fundamental limitations imposed by non-isometric encodings on the accuracy of quantum error correction and implementation of logical operations. We apply our theory to analyze GKP and tiger codes under energy constraints, and discuss the implications to holography.
one_liner: A theory of non-isometric approximate QEC quantifies how finite-energy encodings limit correction accuracy and logical operations.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core experimental issue for continuous-variable codes: real codewords are energy-limited and therefore non-ideal.
detailed_summary: The work develops a systematic approximate quantum error correction framework for non-isometric encodings. It quantifies fundamental limits that non-isometric code maps impose on recovery accuracy and logical gate implementation. The theory is applied to finite-energy GKP and tiger codes, with additional implications discussed for holographic quantum gravity.
main_result: The paper derives general performance bounds for non-isometric quantum codes and applies them to GKP and tiger-code error correction under energy constraints.
application_angle: The results help set realistic error-correction and gate-design expectations for experimentally realizable bosonic and continuous-variable hardware.

title: Temporal glide symmetry enforces a parity sideband selection rule in scalar bulk media
abstract: Symmetry is a powerful way to control coupling between photonic mode families. In spatially periodic structures, glide symmetry can protect band contacts and suppress stop bands. Here we show a different role for temporal glide, a spatiotemporal counterpart combining reflection with a half-period time translation. In a scalar time-modulated trilayer waveguide, temporal glide imposes an exact selection rule linking frequency conversion to transverse-mode symmetry: the parity content of every Floquet eigenstate alternates with sideband index, up to a state-dependent sign. In scattering, this means that a mode of definite parity can emit only into the opposite transverse parity at odd sidebands and into the same parity at even sidebands. We verify the rule directly in bulk Floquet eigenstates and in finite-section time-domain simulations. An incident odd waveguide mode is converted into an even frequency sideband, while all symmetry-forbidden output channels at the analysed sidebands are suppressed to numerically negligible values. Rather than acting as a temporal copy of spatial-glide band sticking, temporal glide provides a distinct symmetry principle for converting electromagnetic energy between selected modes and frequencies.
one_liner: Temporal glide symmetry enforces parity-selective Floquet sideband conversion in a time-modulated trilayer waveguide.
why_it_matters: It gives a symmetry rule for routing optical energy between chosen transverse modes and frequency sidebands in time-varying photonic media.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes a scalar time-modulated trilayer waveguide with temporal glide symmetry, combining reflection with a half-period time translation. It derives an exact Floquet selection rule where transverse-mode parity alternates with sideband index, producing parity-dependent frequency conversion. Bulk eigenstate calculations and finite-section time-domain simulations show allowed odd-to-even sideband conversion while forbidden channels are numerically suppressed.
main_result: The paper demonstrates a temporal-glide-enforced parity sideband selection rule for controlled mode-frequency conversion.
application_angle: The rule can guide design of dynamic photonic components for selective frequency conversion, mode routing, and spatiotemporal signal processing.

title: Generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian for Projective Quantum Feature Maps
abstract: Projected quantum feature maps provide a strategy for using quantum processors as feature generators for classical machine-learning models. Building on counterdiabatic Ising-glass and one-dimensional Heisenberg PQFMs, we introduce a generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian-based PQFM that provides a unified way to encode classical features through local Pauli fields and pairwise two-qubit Pauli interactions. This construction allows distinct classical variables to be embedded along different Pauli axes of the same qubit, increasing the information density of shallow circuits while remaining compatible with hardware constraints. We develop and implement these methods in pqfmlib, a publicly available Python library for constructing, executing, and benchmarking Hamiltonian-based PQFMs.We then benchmark the generalized Hamiltonian PQFMs against reference PQFMs on four biomedical classification datasets under a nested cross-validation protocol with paired statistical tests. Quantum features are generated using both IBM quantum processors with up to 156 qubits and statevector simulations. Our results show that the generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian family provides the most consistent pattern of statistically supported gains over matched classical baselines, although the performance of all methods depends on the dataset, encoding strategy, measured observables, and hardware conditions. These findings support generalized Hamiltonian PQFMs as a promising route toward near-term quantum utility.
one_liner: A generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian PQFM encodes denser classical features and is benchmarked on IBM hardware and simulations.
why_it_matters: It targets near-term quantum machine-learning utility with shallow, hardware-compatible feature maps tested on real biomedical datasets.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a generalized Hamiltonian family for projected quantum feature maps using local Pauli fields and pairwise two-qubit Pauli interactions. The construction embeds distinct classical variables along different Pauli axes on the same qubit, increasing feature density in shallow circuits. Implemented in pqfmlib, the methods are benchmarked with nested cross-validation on four biomedical datasets using statevector simulation and IBM processors up to 156 qubits.
main_result: The generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian PQFMs show the most consistent statistically supported gains over matched classical baselines among the tested PQFM families.
application_angle: The approach provides a practical software-and-hardware path for evaluating quantum feature generators on near-term processors.

title: To Cool, or Not to Cool? Displacement Sensing with Hot Quantum States
abstract: Quantum-enhanced displacement sensing with bosonic systems is typically formulated assuming that the oscillator is cooled close to its ground state before nonclassical probe preparation. We investigate whether such near-ground-state initialization is necessary, or whether sensitive probes can instead be generated directly from thermal states. We analyze hot quantum probes produced by squeezing, number-raising, and Schrödinger-cat-state generation applied to thermal inputs. We identify two distinct mechanisms by which thermal mixedness can remain compatible with enhanced displacement sensitivity. First, projecting a mixed probe onto a definite parity sector removes the usual thermal suppression of the displacement quantum Fisher information, which can then increase with initial thermal occupation. Second, coherent superpositions of opposite displacements can retain sensitivity through coherence between their displaced components, even when the underlying state is mixed. We use these two mechanisms to classify hot-state protocols according to whether their sensitivity comes from parity selection, coherence between displaced components, or both. Finally, we formulate an experimentally relevant optimization problem comparing initial cooling with direct hot-state preparation under realistic decoherence and show that complete cooling is not universally optimal. Our results establish hot-state engineering as a route to quantum-enhanced bosonic displacement sensing without mandatory ground-state initialization.
one_liner: Hot squeezed, number-raised, and cat-like bosonic probes can retain quantum-enhanced displacement sensitivity without full cooling.
why_it_matters: It challenges the assumption that bosonic sensors must start near the ground state, potentially reducing cooling overhead in quantum sensing.
detailed_summary: The paper studies displacement sensing protocols that generate nonclassical probes directly from thermal oscillator states. It identifies parity projection and coherence between displaced components as mechanisms that preserve or enhance displacement quantum Fisher information despite mixedness. An optimization including cooling, hot-state preparation, and decoherence shows that complete cooling is not always the best sensing strategy.
main_result: The work shows that hot quantum states can deliver enhanced displacement sensitivity through parity selection, displaced-component coherence, or both.
application_angle: This could simplify quantum-enhanced force, motion, or field sensors by relaxing ground-state cooling requirements.

title: Quantum Simulation of Spin-Dependent Electron Transfer in a Synthetic Chiral Lattice with a Trapped Ion
abstract: Electron transfer through chiral structures can exhibit spin asymmetry, known as the chiral-induced spin selectivity effect, whose microscopic origin remains an open question. While path-interference within the chiral moiety has been proposed as a key mechanism, its experimental validation requires precise and versatile tunability of system parameters. Here we implement a programmable quantum simulation of spin-dependent electron transfer in a donor--chiral-bridge--acceptor model using a trapped ion. The bridge is encoded in internal states of the ion with tunable nearest- and next-nearest-neighbor couplings, while donor and acceptor states are coupled via a spectator bosonic motional mode. We observe spin-dependent interference within the bridge, and further reveal spin-dependence in donor-to-acceptor transfer dynamics, controlled by amplitude and phase of the coupling parameter. Our results identify interference among spin-dependent pathways as a microscopic origin of spin-dependent transfer, and open a route toward quantum simulations of complex chiral lattices with multi-level and bosonic degrees of freedom.
one_liner: A trapped ion simulates spin-dependent electron transfer through a synthetic chiral donor-bridge-acceptor lattice.
why_it_matters: It provides a programmable hardware probe of chiral-induced spin selectivity mechanisms with tunable interference pathways.
detailed_summary: The experiment implements a donor-chiral-bridge-acceptor model in a trapped ion, encoding the bridge in internal levels with tunable nearest- and next-nearest-neighbor couplings. Donor and acceptor states are coupled through a spectator motional mode, enabling controlled study of transfer dynamics. Measurements reveal spin-dependent interference inside the bridge and spin-dependent donor-to-acceptor transfer set by coupling amplitude and phase.
main_result: The trapped-ion simulator demonstrates controllable spin-dependent pathway interference and transfer dynamics in a chiral electron-transfer model.
application_angle: The platform can test microscopic models of chiral-induced spin selectivity relevant to molecular electronics, spintronics, and chemistry.

title: An integrated ultrahigh vacuum cluster tool for diamond surface science and single nitrogen-vacancy center measurements
abstract: We present a custom-designed ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) cluster tool developed for studying shallow nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, enabling in situ diamond surface preparation, characterization, and single NV center dynamics measurements within a single connected platform. The system combines a surface science chamber for controlled surface modification and analysis with a cryogenic confocal microscope chamber dedicated to NV spin and optical measurements. This integrated approach enables a direct correlation between diamond surface chemistry and the resulting NV spin and charge properties. The instrument provides a versatile platform for systematic studies of surface-induced decoherence mechanisms and charge dynamics for shallow NV centers, and establishes a pathway toward reproducible surface engineering for quantum sensing applications.
one_liner: An integrated UHV cluster tool links diamond surface preparation directly to cryogenic single-NV optical and spin measurements.
why_it_matters: It enables reproducible studies of how diamond surface chemistry controls shallow NV decoherence and charge stability for sensors.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a custom ultrahigh-vacuum cluster platform combining diamond surface preparation and analysis with a cryogenic confocal microscope for single NV measurements. Keeping the process in one connected tool allows direct correlation of surface modifications with NV spin, optical, and charge dynamics. The system is designed for systematic studies of surface-induced decoherence in shallow NV centers.
main_result: The authors demonstrate an integrated platform for in situ diamond surface science and single-NV cryogenic spin-optical characterization.
application_angle: The instrument supports controlled surface engineering needed for robust shallow-NV magnetometry and other diamond quantum sensors.

title: Implementation of two-qubit Rydberg operations on neutral Rb-87 atoms in systems with different intermediate states
abstract: This work presents an experimental setup for implementing two-qubit operations on neutral atoms ($^{87}$Rb) with the possibility of using two different Rydberg excitation schemes. One of them uses 5P$_{1/2}$ as the intermediate level and applies the second-stage beam locally to the addressed atoms. The second scheme uses the 6P$_{3/2}$ level; in this scheme, the particles to be entangled are moved to a separate zone through which both Rydberg beams pass. The advantages and limitations of both schemes are analyzed. Based on numerical modeling performed with a Julia package developed by the authors, it is demonstrated that the spatial configuration has a greater effect on quantum-operation fidelity than the choice of intermediate level. An experimental implementation of the scheme using the 6P$_{3/2}$ level is demonstrated, making it possible to achieve a two-qubit operation fidelity of 94%.
one_liner: Neutral Rb-87 two-qubit Rydberg operations are compared across excitation schemes, with a 6P-based implementation reaching 94% fidelity.
why_it_matters: It clarifies hardware tradeoffs for neutral-atom entangling operations and shows spatial configuration can dominate gate fidelity.
detailed_summary: The work compares two Rydberg excitation schemes for neutral rubidium-87 atoms: one using 5P1/2 with local second-stage addressing and one using 6P3/2 in a separate entangling zone. Numerical modeling with the authors' Julia package indicates that spatial configuration affects operation fidelity more strongly than the intermediate-state choice. The experimental 6P3/2 implementation demonstrates a two-qubit operation fidelity of 94%.
main_result: The paper experimentally realizes a 6P3/2-mediated neutral-atom two-qubit Rydberg operation with 94% fidelity.
application_angle: The comparison informs architecture choices for scalable neutral-atom quantum processors using movable atoms and Rydberg entanglement zones.

title: All about quantum error correction: distillation, mitigation, self-correction and beyond
abstract: In this work, it is shown that many quantum error-manipulating techniques, such as distillation, error mitigation, and dynamical decoupling, are special cases of the most general framework for quantum error correction. This unifying perspective is achieved by extending quantum error correction to include state-adaptive and channel-adaptive settings, as well as multi-stage coding scenarios. Based on this insight, a model of self-correcting quantum memory is also proposed. This work clarifies the relationship among these techniques and illustrates, through explicit constructions, how the unified perspective can guide the design of reliable quantum information systems.
one_liner: A unifying theory frames distillation, mitigation, dynamical decoupling, and self-correction as generalized quantum error correction.
why_it_matters: It clarifies how disparate reliability techniques can be composed and compared when designing fault-tolerant quantum information systems.
detailed_summary: The paper extends quantum error correction to state-adaptive, channel-adaptive, and multi-stage coding settings. Within this broader formalism, techniques such as distillation, error mitigation, and dynamical decoupling appear as special cases rather than separate tools. The authors use this viewpoint to propose a model of self-correcting quantum memory and to give explicit constructions for reliable quantum information processing.
main_result: The main result is a generalized QEC framework that subsumes multiple error-manipulation protocols and motivates a self-correcting memory model.
application_angle: The framework can guide practical architecture choices for more reliable quantum processors, memories, and networked quantum systems.

title: Link-Free Multi-Node Timing Synchronization for Scalable Quantum Networking
abstract: Precise timing synchronization is essential for distributed quantum networking, enabling entanglement distribution, quantum teleportation, and entanglement swapping across remote nodes. Existing synchronization architectures rely on dedicated timing-distribution infrastructure, most notably White Rabbit networks, which constrain topology, scalability, and deployment in free-space and satellite environments. Here we demonstrate link-free synchronization of quantum network nodes using independently operating miniature rubidium atomic clocks and computational post-processing. We validate the approach on a deployed metropolitan-scale telecom fiber network spanning three geographically separated nodes. Following drift correction, atomic-clock-based synchronization achieves timing performance approaching that of a White Rabbit benchmark and remains stable over continuous 8-hour operation. As a stringent test of quantum-network functionality, we observe Hong-Ou-Mandel interference across spatially separated nodes with visibility exceeding 70%, statistically equivalent to that obtained using dedicated White Rabbit timing links. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first observation of quantum interference across a deployed metropolitan-scale telecom fiber network synchronized entirely without dedicated timing-transfer infrastructure. These results establish atomic-clock-based synchronization as a scalable, topology-independent alternative to conventional timing-distribution architectures and a practical pathway toward terrestrial, airborne, and space-based quantum networks where dedicated timing links are unavailable.
one_liner: Miniature rubidium clocks synchronize three metro fiber quantum nodes without dedicated timing links and still enable HOM interference.
why_it_matters: Removing timing-transfer infrastructure makes deployed, free-space, airborne, and satellite quantum networks easier to scale and reconfigure.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates link-free synchronization of geographically separated quantum-network nodes using independently running miniature rubidium atomic clocks plus post-processing drift correction. Tests on a deployed metropolitan telecom fiber network show timing performance approaching a White Rabbit benchmark over continuous 8-hour operation. As a quantum-network validation, the authors observe Hong-Ou-Mandel interference between separated nodes with visibility above 70%, statistically matching the dedicated-timing-link case.
main_result: Three separated nodes were synchronized without dedicated timing transfer while preserving high-visibility two-photon interference.
application_angle: This provides a practical synchronization route for scalable terrestrial, airborne, and space quantum networks where timing links are costly or unavailable.

title: OQMD: Single-Qubit Rotation Control Improves Low-CNOT Multiclass Quantum Classification
abstract: Near-term variational classifiers incur substantial error and latency from two-qubit gates, yet practitioners often assume that additional entangling depth is the default route to higher accuracy. This work studies Optimal Quantum Measurement Decoding (OQMD): optimizing how quantum outcomes are mapped to classical labels by training a readout layer before measurement, jointly with the variational circuit, without adding CNOTs. Experiments use trainable triple single-qubit rotations as one concrete, hardware-native realization of OQMD; other single-qubit parametrizations fit the same classical outer loop. On the Iris benchmark with a 30-point stratified test split, the best observed 0-CNOT configuration with OQMD reaches 83.33\% accuracy, with a 96\% at 9 CNOTs, exceeding the best 18-CNOT controls (56.67\%) and the best 18-CNOT configuration with OQMD (66.67\%) under a common protocol. A six-point CNOT-depth series from 0 to 18 (fixed optimizer, iteration budget, random-seed count, and ZXZ readout) shows that the highest raw scores need not occur at the largest template, so aggregate complexity is not summarized by CNOT count alone. Because run-level accuracies are discrete and non-Gaussian, we emphasize best-observed scores and, where a global comparison of pooled runs is required, Mann--Whitney $U$ tests rather than parametric tests on means. Across architectures, OQMD shows statistically consistent but magnitude-dependent gains: large peak lifts on minimal circuits coexist with a small pooled mean shift on complex 18-CNOT runs ($p\approx 0.03$) that is not ``universal'' in the sense of uniformly large practical effects.%
one_liner: OQMD improves multiclass variational classification by training single-qubit readout rotations instead of adding CNOT depth.
why_it_matters: It targets a near-term hardware bottleneck by trading error-prone entangling gates for cheaper, hardware-native single-qubit control.
detailed_summary: The paper studies Optimal Quantum Measurement Decoding, where a trainable readout layer maps quantum outcomes to labels jointly with the variational circuit. Using trainable triple single-qubit rotations, the authors report an 83.33% best observed accuracy at 0 CNOTs and 96% at 9 CNOTs on a 30-point Iris test split. The CNOT-depth sweep indicates that larger entangling templates do not necessarily yield better peak accuracy under the tested protocol.
main_result: A low-CNOT classifier with OQMD outperformed higher-CNOT controls in the reported Iris benchmark experiments.
application_angle: The method may reduce latency and two-qubit-gate error for practical NISQ classification workloads.

title: Beam shifts and eigenpolarisations for the reflection of vortex beams from homogeneous magnetic surfaces
abstract: Probing surfaces with optical beams is a powerful technique utilised for material characterisation, and more recently, measuring surface magnetisation with vortex beams has opened new avenues by utilising OAM beams for metrology. In this paper, we focus on extending the theoretical framework of unit order vortex beam reflection to spatially homogeneous magnetic surfaces, and derive expressions for the Goos-Hänchen (GH) and Imbert-Federov (IF) shifts. We calculate closed form expressions for the plane wave eigenpolarisations, polarisations which remain in the same vectorial direction after reflection, for both dielectric and homogeneous magnetic surfaces. These are found by extending the singularimetry formalism for dielectric surfaces, where the position of the phase vortex can be analytically approximated by taking into account the finite beam width, to homogeneous magnetic surfaces. From this we find the eigenpolarisations of the unit order vortex beam for reflection from both dielectric and homogeneous magnetic surfaces. We discuss with relation to the analytical forms of the Jones matrices for GH and IF shifts the additional reflection behaviour observed for homogeneous magnetic surfaces.
one_liner: A theory of vortex-beam reflection from homogeneous magnetic surfaces derives GH/IF shifts and eigenpolarisations.
why_it_matters: It extends optical OAM metrology tools for magnetic surface characterization and polarization-sensitive beam-shift measurements.
detailed_summary: The paper extends the theoretical treatment of unit-order vortex beam reflection from dielectric to spatially homogeneous magnetic surfaces. It derives expressions for Goos-Hanchen and Imbert-Fedorov shifts and calculates closed-form plane-wave eigenpolarisations. By adapting singularimetry methods to magnetic surfaces, it predicts vortex-beam eigenpolarisations and additional reflection behavior tied to magnetic Jones matrices.
main_result: The authors derive analytic beam-shift and eigenpolarisation expressions for vortex beams reflected from homogeneous magnetic surfaces.
application_angle: The results support optical sensing schemes for surface magnetization and material characterization using structured light.

title: Dynamically frozen long-distance entanglement via non-Hermitian PT-symmetric systems
abstract: In distributed quantum networks, interacting spin systems can mediate the generation of highly entangled links between distant nodes. We investigate the role of effective parity-time (PT)-symmetric non-Hermitian spin-1/2 bulks weakly coupled to two quantum links, obtained due to the environmental interactions affecting both the bulk and the links. Focusing on effective non-Hermitian nearest-neighbor (NN) Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) models, we analyze how non-Hermiticity influences the dynamical formation of long-distance entanglement (LDE). For a paradigmatic model consisting of a quantum XX bulk subjected to imaginary staggered magnetic fields, we analytically determine the exceptional points arising from the resulting bulk-mediated interactions between the links. Combining analytical and numerical methods, we demonstrate that an initially fully separable state can dynamically evolve into highly entangled link states near these exceptional points in the broken regime. Further, after optimizing over time and system parameters, near-unit time-averaged entanglement between the links emerges under weak imaginary magnetic fields and bulk-link couplings, which cannot be attained in the corresponding Hermitian systems. Moreover, the non-Hermitian dynamics exhibit a freezing of high entanglement in the vicinity of exceptional points, a feature absent in Hermitian counterparts. We also identify regimes of long-range interaction strengths that yield a higher time-averaged entanglement than the corresponding NN models. Furthermore, we establish that LDE persists in the stationary regime, highlighting the promise of engineered non-Hermitian dynamics for realizing robust and frozen entangled links in quantum networks.
one_liner: PT-symmetric non-Hermitian spin chains can dynamically generate and freeze long-distance entanglement near exceptional points.
why_it_matters: Frozen high entanglement could make mediated quantum-network links more robust than comparable Hermitian spin-bulk schemes.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze spin-1/2 links weakly coupled through effective PT-symmetric non-Hermitian SSH-type bulks arising from environmental interactions. For an XX bulk with imaginary staggered magnetic fields, they identify exceptional points in the induced link interactions. Near these points, initially separable states can evolve into highly entangled link states, with near-unit time-averaged entanglement and dynamical freezing that is absent in Hermitian counterparts.
main_result: Engineered PT-symmetric bulk dynamics produce robust, long-distance entanglement that can remain frozen near exceptional points.
application_angle: The mechanism suggests a route to stable entangled links for distributed quantum networks using engineered open-system dynamics.

title: Simultaneous Estimation of Partial-Transpose Moments with Active Memory Independent of the Moment Order
abstract: We study the simultaneous estimation of partial-transpose moments $p_j(ρ_{AB})=\mathrm{Tr}[(ρ_{AB}^{T_B})^j]$, $j=2,\ldots,K$, of an unknown bipartite $n$-qubit state from independent copies under an explicit active-memory constraint. We give a sequential qubit-reuse realization of the partial-transpose permutation that uses at most $2n+1$ active qubits, independent of $K$, and estimates all moments $p_2,\ldots,p_K$ to uniform additive error $ε$ with total copy complexity $O(K\log K/ε^2)$. We also prove two converse bounds. First, any uniformly accurate simultaneous estimator requires $Ω(K/ε^2)$ copies in the worst case. Second, the same scaling holds on an explicit isospectral two-qubit negative-partial-transpose (NPT) family whose ordinary moments are constant while the partial-transpose moments vary. These results characterize the copy complexity of the partial-transpose moment hierarchy up to a logarithmic factor and extend simultaneous nonlinear-functional estimation from ordinary state powers to partial-transpose spectral data under active quantum memory independent of the target moment order.
one_liner: A qubit-reuse protocol estimates partial-transpose moments up to order K using active memory independent of K.
why_it_matters: It makes entanglement-spectrum diagnostics more feasible on memory-limited quantum devices and network nodes.
detailed_summary: The paper gives a sequential implementation of partial-transpose permutation measurements for estimating moments p2 through pK of an unknown bipartite n-qubit state. The protocol uses at most 2n+1 active qubits regardless of K and reaches uniform additive error epsilon with O(K log K / epsilon^2) copies. Converse bounds show Omega(K / epsilon^2) copies are necessary in the worst case and for an explicit two-qubit NPT family.
main_result: The authors achieve simultaneous partial-transpose moment estimation with K-independent active memory and near-optimal copy complexity.
application_angle: This supports practical entanglement certification and benchmarking under limited coherent memory resources.

title: Spin mixing induced dynamics of spinor solitons in $F=1$ Bose Einstein condensates
abstract: We explore soliton interactions in a homogeneous spinor $F=1$ Bose Einstein Condensate (BEC) in the presence of a magnetic field, focusing on dark bright dark and bright dark bright configurations. We investigate how these interactions depend on the phase differences among bright solitons and their influence during the dynamics. Our findings align with prior non spinor results, i.e., repulsion among in phase bright solitons and attraction among out of phase pairs in self repulsive atomic BECs. The potential bright soliton attraction, added to the short range repulsion of dark dark soliton interactions, can lead to bound states. However, we find that these bound states break in the presence of spinor interactions due to the particle exchange dynamics between the hyperfine states of the components. Additonally, we develop an effective classical model to describe the soliton dynamics, using a Lagrangian approach. The accuracy of the model is tested by comparing it against numerical simulations. Our results suggest that the proposed model captures the essential features of soliton behavior in the presence of spin interactions, and provides congruent soliton trajectories and interspecies particle exchange dynamics in most of the cases.
one_liner: Spinor interactions destabilize bound dark-bright soliton pairs through hyperfine-state particle exchange in F=1 condensates.
why_it_matters: The work clarifies controllable nonlinear dynamics in multicomponent quantum gases, relevant to matter-wave routing and coherent atom-optic devices.
detailed_summary: The paper studies dark-bright-dark and bright-dark-bright soliton configurations in homogeneous F=1 Bose-Einstein condensates under a magnetic field. Numerical simulations show the expected phase-dependent attraction or repulsion of bright solitons, but spinor particle-exchange dynamics can break otherwise stable bound states. A reduced classical Lagrangian model reproduces key soliton trajectories and interspecies exchange behavior across most tested cases.
main_result: Spin mixing is shown to break phase-bound soliton states, with an effective classical model capturing the dominant trajectories and exchange dynamics.
application_angle: Understanding these interactions supports engineered matter-wave structures for atom-optic and coherent nonlinear-wave technologies.

title: Dose-efficient Quantum Phase Estimation in Lossy Optical Interferometry
abstract: Optical interferometry is a cornerstone technique for precise phase measurements across various fields. In many applications, for example, biological imaging, it often necessitates stringent limits on light intensity to prevent adverse effects on light-sensitive samples, a condition known as dose-limited regimes. Maximizing the precision per dose is therefore crucial. In quantum metrology, quantum correlations enable high precision in phase estimation while adhering to dose constraints. Nevertheless, photon loss, including absorption by a sample, substantially diminishes the benefits of quantum enhancement in interferometry. In this work, we experimentally investigate a dose-efficient approach to quantum phase estimation using sequential strategies in the presence of loss. Performance of sequential strategies with and without control is evaluated through quantum Fisher information (QFI) per dose. Experimental results show that both sequential strategies exceed the classical limit and outperform the parallel strategy using unbalanced N00N states. Notably, the control-enhanced sequential strategy attains superior QFI per dose, approaching the quantum limit. These results highlight the promise of sequential strategy for imaging and sensing in resource-constrained scenarios, marking a significant step toward practical and efficient quantum metrology in lossy environments.
one_liner: Sequential optical phase estimation beats classical dose efficiency under loss and approaches the quantum limit with feedback control.
why_it_matters: It directly targets low-light sensing and imaging where samples are fragile and photon budget, not raw precision, is the limiting resource.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally evaluate sequential quantum phase-estimation strategies in lossy optical interferometry using quantum Fisher information per dose as the figure of merit. Both uncontrolled and control-enhanced sequential protocols exceed the classical limit and outperform a parallel strategy based on unbalanced N00N states. The feedback-controlled version gives the best dose efficiency, approaching the quantum limit despite loss.
main_result: A control-enhanced sequential phase-estimation protocol achieves superior QFI per dose beyond classical and unbalanced-N00N parallel benchmarks.
application_angle: The approach is suited to biological imaging and other light-sensitive sensing tasks where phase information must be extracted with minimal photon exposure.

title: A new class of degenerate solutions to the massless Dirac equation and their potential applications in optical memories
abstract: In this article, we present a novel class of degenerate solutions to the massless Dirac equation, corresponding to a wide variety of electromagnetic 4-potentials and fields, including both zero field and circularly polarized electromagnetic waves. An interesting property of these solutions is that the spin of the particles rotates in synchronization with the electric and magnetic fields of the electromagnetic waves. These results could be utilized for the development of optical memories based on materials supporting massless Dirac fermions, such as graphene.
one_liner: A degenerate family of massless Dirac solutions links synchronized spin rotation to optical fields in Dirac materials.
why_it_matters: The proposal points toward optically addressable memory mechanisms in graphene-like systems, though it is primarily theoretical at this stage.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a new class of degenerate solutions to the massless Dirac equation for a broad set of electromagnetic four-potentials and fields. Cases include zero field and circularly polarized waves, with particle spin rotating synchronously with the electromagnetic field. The authors suggest that this controllable spin-field relation may be useful in optical memory concepts based on materials hosting massless Dirac fermions.
main_result: The work identifies degenerate Dirac solutions whose spin dynamics can lock to circularly polarized electromagnetic waves.
application_angle: If realizable in graphene or related materials, the effect could inform optically controlled memory elements or field-sensitive material responses.

title: Modeling light-matter coupled systems with neural quantum states
abstract: Recent advances in cold atom manipulation enable the study of many-body systems where short-range interactions between neighboring atoms coexist with long-range interactions mediated by photons. Such a combination of interactions makes a theoretical approach challenging beyond mean-field methods. In this work, we develop a neural quantum state based approach to study these systems numerically. We introduce a neural-network architecture capable of handling hybrid Hilbert spaces with large local bosonic dimensions in strongly interacting spin-photon systems. We benchmark this approach on a model of a two-dimensional lattice of Rydberg atoms coupled to a photon mode. The superradiant ground states found in the large spin-photon coupling regime allow us to demonstrate the efficiency of the method in the presence of high photon occupation. Furthermore, the ability to capture spin-spin and spin-photon correlations leads us to observe quantitative deviations in the ground state phase boundaries with respect to mean-field theory. The method extends to other systems with a similar hybrid Hilbert space structure, such as spin-phonon systems, and provides a scalable framework for investigating their ground state properties.
one_liner: Neural quantum states are adapted to simulate strongly coupled spin-photon many-body systems with large bosonic occupations.
why_it_matters: The method gives hardware theorists a scalable tool for regimes where photon-mediated interactions and high photon number defeat mean-field treatments.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a neural-network variational ansatz designed for hybrid Hilbert spaces combining spin degrees of freedom with large local bosonic dimensions. They benchmark it on a two-dimensional Rydberg-atom lattice coupled to a photon mode and resolve superradiant ground states with high photon occupation. By capturing spin-spin and spin-photon correlations, the method predicts phase-boundary shifts relative to mean-field theory.
platform_or_system: Rydberg atom lattice coupled to a photon mode
main_result: A neural quantum state architecture efficiently models correlated spin-photon ground states and reveals quantitative deviations from mean-field phase boundaries.
application_angle: This supports design and validation of cavity- or photon-mediated quantum simulators and may extend to spin-phonon hardware platforms.

title: Quantum-Classical Hierarchical Equations of Motion
abstract: We develop a quantum-classical hierarchical equations of motion (QC-HEOM) approach for simulating non-Markovian open quantum systems. The method combines the ensemble-averaged classical path reference of the quantum-classical path integral formalism with a hierarchy of auxiliary quantum influence functionals. By incorporating thermal fluctuations through an ensemble average over reference trajectories, the hierarchy is required to represent only the residual quantum memory associated with the imaginary part of the bath response function. Consequently, unlike conventional hierarchical equations of motion, QC-HEOM does not require Matsubara or Padé expansions of the thermal kernel and exhibits only weak temperature dependence of the hierarchy size. Furthermore, because thermal fluctuations are supplied through reference classical trajectories, the framework naturally extends beyond harmonic baths and enables the incorporation of anharmonic and molecular environments through externally generated trajectories. We derive the formalism and demonstrate its exactness for a harmonic bath. Applications to an asymmetric spin-boson model and the seven-site Fenna--Matthews--Olson complex illustrate the accuracy of QC-HEOM. It reproduces benchmark quasi-adiabatic path integral and hierarchical equations of motion results while requiring substantially fewer auxiliary objects, particularly at low temperatures. These results establish QC-HEOM as an efficient framework for treating residual quantum memory in quantum-classical descriptions of open-system dynamics. The separation of thermal fluctuations from residual quantum memory through the use of Wigner trajectories provides an approximate route toward hierarchical treatments of complex anharmonic environments that are inaccessible to conventional HEOM approaches.
one_liner: QC-HEOM separates classical thermal fluctuations from residual quantum memory to simulate non-Markovian open-system dynamics efficiently.
why_it_matters: Efficient non-Markovian modeling is important for quantum devices and molecular quantum networks operating in structured, finite-temperature environments.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a quantum-classical hierarchical equations of motion framework that uses classical reference trajectories for thermal fluctuations and reserves the hierarchy for residual quantum memory. For harmonic baths, the method is derived as exact and avoids Matsubara or Pade thermal-kernel expansions. Benchmarks on an asymmetric spin-boson model and the Fenna-Matthews-Olson complex reproduce established results with fewer auxiliary objects, especially at low temperature.
main_result: QC-HEOM matches benchmark open-system dynamics while reducing hierarchy size by shifting thermal fluctuations into classical trajectory ensembles.
application_angle: The framework can help model decoherence and transport in quantum hardware, molecular environments, and communication-relevant quantum interfaces.

title: Certification of the genuine resolution of photon number resolving detectors
abstract: Photon-number-resolving (PNR) detectors are essential components of photonic quantum technologies, yet thus far, no practical metric exists to certify how many photons they can genuinely resolve in a single measurement. Here we introduce an operational framework for quantifying the capability of a PNR detector to distinguish between different numbers of photons, i.e. its genuine resolution. In turn, we develop a practical and scalable protocol for certifying the genuine resolution of a detector, which is based on coherent state probes. We apply the method to a 28-pixel photon-number-resolving superconducting nanowire single-photon detector (PNR-SNSPD) and certify genuine four-outcome resolution. Our work highlights the critical requirements in terms of detector efficiency towards achieving high genuine resolution. This approach provides an operational benchmark for PNR detectors and fills a crucial gap in the characterization of photonic quantum devices.
one_liner: A coherent-state certification protocol quantifies how many photon-number outcomes a PNR detector genuinely resolves.
why_it_matters: It supplies a practical benchmark for a key component in photonic quantum computing, sensing, and communication systems.
detailed_summary: The authors define an operational metric for the genuine photon-number resolution of photon-number-resolving detectors. They propose a scalable certification protocol using coherent-state probes and apply it to a 28-pixel PNR superconducting nanowire single-photon detector. The experiment certifies genuine four-outcome resolution and identifies detector efficiency as a critical requirement for higher certified resolution.
main_result: The protocol certifies genuine four-outcome photon-number resolution in a multiplexed PNR-SNSPD using coherent-state probes.
application_angle: Reliable resolution certification improves component qualification for photonic quantum processors, quantum networks, and low-light optical measurements.

title: Quantum sensing through bosonic-fermionic Bell-state transitions in two-photon interference
abstract: Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference has become a central resource for quantum sensing and metrology owing to its sensitivity to temporal delay and photon indistinguishability. However, existing HOM-based sensing schemes generally rely on inserting a sample into one arm of the interferometer, making the measurement vulnerable to optical loss, alignment instability, and bandwidth-dependent distortion of the interference profile. Here, we demonstrate a symmetry-controlled quantum sensing scheme based on continuous transitions between symmetric (bosonic-like) and antisymmetric (fermionic-like) Bell states in two-photon interference. By imprinting a geometric phase onto the classical pump beam and transferring it to polarization-entangled photons generated via spontaneous parametric down-conversion, we coherently tune the exchange symmetry of the entangled state without altering the temporal or spectral indistinguishability of the photons. The HOM response evolves continuously from bunching to antibunching with a sine square phase dependence, producing a coincidence modulation of approximately 10 * 10^4 counts s^-1 counts/s. In contrast to conventional HOM sensing, the phase-modulation linewidth remains fixed at pi/2, independent of photon bandwidth. Using a birefringent crystal placed directly in the pump beam, we measure thermo-dispersive birefringence with a resolution of the order of 10^{-6} over a broad temperature range. Our results establish exchange symmetry as a controllable resource for robust quantum sensing and symmetry-engineered photonic quantum information processing.
one_liner: A HOM interferometer senses birefringence by tuning two-photon exchange symmetry via pump-imprinted geometric phase.
why_it_matters: It moves HOM sensing from sample-in-arm delay changes to pump-beam phase control, reducing loss/alignment sensitivity while preserving photon bandwidth response.
detailed_summary: The experiment imprints a geometric phase on the classical pump and transfers it to polarization-entangled photon pairs generated by SPDC. This coherently tunes the two-photon state between symmetric bosonic-like and antisymmetric fermionic-like Bell-state behavior without changing temporal or spectral indistinguishability. The HOM coincidence response switches continuously from bunching to antibunching with a sine-squared phase dependence, and a pump-path birefringent crystal enables thermo-dispersive birefringence sensing at about 10^-6 resolution.
main_result: The work demonstrates symmetry-controlled HOM sensing with fixed pi/2 phase-modulation linewidth and approximately 10 x 10^4 counts/s coincidence modulation.
application_angle: The approach supports more robust photonic metrology for birefringence and phase-sensitive sensing where inserting samples into interferometer arms is impractical.

title: Spin counting via projection noise measurement of mesoscopic solid-state spin ensemble
abstract: Quantum projection noise is the fundamental noise source for the population measurement of spin ensembles. While projection-noise-limited measurements have been extensively studied in atomic systems, corresponding experiments on solid-state spin ensembles remain challenging due to dominant classical readout noise. Here, we report direct measurement of the quantum projection noise of mesoscopic ensembles of nitrogen-vacancy (NV) spin defects at room temperature. Our experiment is enabled by a high optically-detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) contrast of over 20% for a single crystallographic orientation of the defect spins, obtained by combining polarization-selective optical excitation with spin-to-charge conversion. We use our protocol to demonstrate projection noise measurements and spin counting from nanoscale NV ensembles of up to 43 spins. We further demonstrate that the protocol allows for significant gains in sensitivity for magnetometry applications without need for cryogenic operation or high bias magnetic fields.
one_liner: Room-temperature NV ensembles are spin-counted by directly measuring quantum projection noise despite classical optical readout noise.
why_it_matters: Projection-noise readout in solid-state spin ensembles can improve nanoscale magnetometry without cryogenics or large bias fields.
detailed_summary: The authors directly measure quantum projection noise in mesoscopic nitrogen-vacancy spin ensembles at room temperature. High ODMR contrast above 20% for a single crystallographic orientation is achieved using polarization-selective optical excitation combined with spin-to-charge conversion. The protocol resolves projection noise and counts nanoscale ensembles containing up to 43 spins, then maps the method to magnetometry sensitivity gains.
main_result: The paper demonstrates direct projection-noise measurement and spin counting for room-temperature NV ensembles up to 43 spins.
application_angle: It provides a practical readout route for higher-sensitivity nanoscale solid-state magnetometers under accessible operating conditions.

title: Experimental realization of the complete seven-phase Anderson-localization landscape
abstract: Anderson localization has evolved far beyond the conventional dichotomy between extended and localized states. Modern localization theory predicts a complete transport hierarchy comprising extended, critical, and localized phases together with all coexistence phases among them, forming a seven-phase Anderson-localization landscape. Despite its fundamental importance, this hierarchy has never been experimentally realized within a single system. Here we realize the complete seven-phase Anderson-localization landscape in a one-dimensional Floquet photonic lattice. By engineering quasiperiodic hopping profiles containing inhomogeneously distributed hopping zeros, we generate critical states and enable their coexistence with extended and localized sectors.
